They also underscore the importance of considering practical implementation factors alongside theoretical frameworks when developing maintenance solutions for the aviation industry. 4.9. Future Research Directions The integration of customization and personalization in aircraft maintenance repre- sents a significant shift in how airlines and service providers manage operational efficiency and safety. While the current frameworks of traditional MRO models and ATSaaS platforms have demonstrated their value, further research is needed to explore emerging technolo- gies, address implementation challenges, and unlock new possibilities for innovation in this domain. Advanced predictive analytics represents a critical area for future studies, focusing on enhancing maintenance capabilities through machine learning and artificial intelligence. This includes developing more accurate failure prediction algorithms for complex systems, Processes 2025, 13, 666 50 of 54 integrating environmental and operational data for nuanced predictive models, and inves- tigating hybrid analytics frameworks that combine predictive and prescriptive approaches for optimized maintenance scheduling. Research into the integration of customization and personalization requires deeper exploration, particularly in developing hybrid models that effectively combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization. Key areas include identifying optimal frameworks for integrating manual customization with data-driven personal- ization, assessing operational and economic impacts on airlines of different sizes, and developing methodologies for dynamically transitioning between approaches based on real-time conditions. The scalability of ATSaaS platforms warrants further investigation as these systems gain broader adoption. Research should focus on evaluating how platforms can efficiently scale predictive maintenance capabilities for large fleets without compromising accuracy, in- vestigating cost-effective solutions for small and medium-sized airlines, and exploring mod- ular design approaches that enable seamless adaptation to growing operational demands. Cybersecurity in aircraft maintenance emerges as a crucial research priority given the increasing reliance on digital tools and data-driven platforms. Studies should assess vulnerabilities in ATSaaS platforms and maintenance technologies, investigate blockchain technology’s potential for enhancing data security and traceability, and design robust contingency plans and resilience frameworks to mitigate cyber risks. Economic viability and return on investment analysis require thorough investigation through cost-benefit analyses of different maintenance models, assessment of ROI for airlines transitioning to ATSaaS platforms, and exploration of financial incentives and business models to encourage adoption among smaller operators. Future research in aircraft maintenance should ultimately aim to enhance the inte- gration of advanced technologies, refine hybrid models, and address challenges related to scalability, cybersecurity, and sustainability. These efforts will contribute to developing innovative, efficient, and environmentally responsible maintenance practices that meet the evolving demands of the aviation industry while improving

input.query: Welche Rolle spielt generative KI im Bereich Predictive Maintenance für Flugzeuge?
input.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den Einsatz generativer KI (z. B. LLMs, Diffusionsmodelle) zur vorausschauenden Instandhaltung in der Luftfahrt. Relevante Teilaspekte sind (1) konkrete Anwendungsfälle entlang des PHM/CBM-Prozesses (Datenaufbereitung, Diagnose/Prognose, Work-Order/Tech-Log), (2) methodische Ansätze zur Kombination von GenAI mit Zeitreihen-/Sensorikmodellen und digitalen Zwillingen, (3) Nachweise zu Performance/ROI/Sicherheitsnutzen, sowie (4) regulatorische/standardbezogene Anforderungen (EASA/FAA, DO-178C/DO-330/DO-200A, ISO/SAE) inkl. Trustworthiness, Traceability und Human Factors.

research_aspects.description: Erfasst konkrete Einsatzpunkte generativer KI (z. B. automatische Auswertung von Tech Logs, Assistenz bei Fehlersuche, Work-Order-Erstellung, Teile-/Wartungsplanung) und verortet sie im Predictive-Maintenance-Prozess in der Luftfahrt.

Sammelt Evidenz zu Leistungsmetriken und Nutzenbelegen (z. B. MTBUR, AOG-Reduktion, False-Alarm-Rate, Turnaround-Time, Kosten/ROI) sowie Grenzen/Trade-offs im Vergleich zu nicht-generativen Ansätzen.

Klärt Anforderungen von EASA/FAA und einschlägige Standards (z. B. DO-178C, DO-330, DO-200A, SAE/ISO) an Tool Qualification, Datenintegrität, Nachvollziehbarkeit und den Einsatz von KI in sicherheitskritischen Instandhaltungsprozessen.

Analysiert Risiken wie Halluzinationen, Bias, Datenqualität, Cybersecurity sowie Anforderungen an Human-in-the-Loop, Erklärbarkeit, Traceability, Auditierbarkeit und organisatorische Governance für den sicheren Betrieb GenAI-gestützter Main

However, the quantity and quality of the data gathered are crucial to the prediction's accuracy because inaccurate or insufficient data might provide erroneous models [12]. Finally, the hybrid approach combines these methods. The recent surge in machine data, combined with the power of deep learning, is significantly improving the accuracy of PdM models. This leads to more targeted the maintenance resources on strategies, focusing they turn components most likely to fail, ultimately leading to optimized maintenance plans [8]. As industrial demands for efficiency and reliability are pushing traditional maintenance methods to their limits, Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) has emerged as a powerful solution in aviation industry [10], in which PdM is also a key element [12]. By analyzing real-time and historical data, PHM can assess aircraft equipment health and predict future issues. Machine learning, a key tool within PHM, utilizes algorithms trained on vast amounts of data to predict equipment health or remaining useful life (RUL) [6]. However, a major hurdle lies in the need for labeled data (data with known outcomes) for training these algorithms [13]. Limited labeled data can significantly restrict the effectiveness of machine learning in PdM [14]. Acquiring the ideal data for machine learning in PdM for aircraft is practically challenging. This data, commonly known as "run-to-failure" data, consists of time-series readings that capture the gradual degradation of a component – from a healthy state all the way to complete failure [15]. The problem lies in the extended timelines involved. For instance, a seemingly minor issue in a critical aircraft part might take months or even years to progress to complete failure under normal operating conditions. However, allowing a component to reach this point is not only unacceptable but also poses a serious safety risk. To prioritize safety, aircraft parts are routinely replaced well before reaching a critical failure state. Researchers are actively addressing this challenge by developing generative AI models that can predict future equipment health even with scarce failure data [16]. In order to improve PdM procedures in the aviation sector and get beyond data constraints, generative AI (GAI) models are an effective technique. These algorithms may create synthetic data that imitates real-world "run-to-failure" circumstances after being trained on available data, even if it is incomplete. This synthetic data can then be used to train other machine learning algorithms for PdM tasks. Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment

A variety of methods are included in generative AI, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and variational autoencoders (VAEs). VAEs generate unique and credible data points by examining underlying patterns in the data [8]. GANs, on the other hand, employ a collaborative competition between two neural networks. One network generates data, while the other strives to differentiate between real and generated data. Abstract Predictive maintenance (PdM) is a critical tool in aviation, promoting sustainability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. However, a significant challenge in implementing predictive maintenance framework is limited run-to-failure data due to frequent preventive maintenance. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: . The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly analyze papers sourced from four scientific databases. These papers focus on predictive maintenance of aircraft systems through generative AI models. The findings of this review examine various aspects of GAI-powered predictive maintenance, including its objectives, the diversity of models employed, areas of application, datasets used for model validation, and the prevailing challenges and emerging trends. The review identified that most commonly used GAI models in aircraft PdM are GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks), VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) or combination of these models. However, knowledge sharing, model integration and specificity are the key challenges associated with the implementation of these models. This strong knowledge -base will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners aiming to improve their knowledge and implementation of GAI- driven models for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. Keywords: Generative AI, Predictive Maintenance (PdM), Aircraft Maintenance, Generative Adversarial Network, Data Augmentation 1. Introduction Maintenance plays a pivotal role in the aviation industry, with its importance deeply rooted in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft operations. Operating in an environment characterized by extreme temperatures, high altitudes, and various forces during flight, aircraft systems undergo age-related degradation and operational wear without regular maintenance. The repercussions of such neglect are severe, leading to system failures that can compromise the safety of passengers and crew on board [1]. Conversely, the financial ramifications of grounded aircraft due to insufficient maintenance are substantial, compelling airlines to prioritize comprehensive maintenance programs [2]. Regular maintenance actions not only strengthen the structural and operational integrity of aircraft but also extend the lifespan of critical equipment, mitigating revenue losses associated with unplanned downtime [3]. According to a report by Statista, the financial burden of unscheduled aircraft maintenance is anticipated to skyrocket in the aviation industry, reaching an estimated annual cost of nearly 39 billion USD by 2035 [4]. This projection is particularly alarming considering the expected surge in the global fleet size, forecasted to almost double from 25,900 to 47,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems

The research questions themselves might have missed crucial aspects or made assumptions due to the evolving nature of the field and the vast number of approaches for developing these that technologies. Moreover, only considering studies combined GAI with predictive maintenance limited the findings' generalizability. This means they might not be applicable to other forms of predictive maintenance techniques. 6. Conclusion This study explored the growing use of Generative AI in transforming how the aviation industry performs predictive maintenance on aircraft. A systematic literature review was conducted, analyzing studies published over the past five years. The focus was on understanding how GAI is the role revolutionizing aircraft PdM. This generative models play, the specific models being used, the areas where GAI is applied, and the datasets employed to validate these models. included The study implemented a semi-automated approach for the literature review, utilizing ASReview and PRISMA techniques. This process identified 16 relevant studies out of a pool of 442. The findings highlight the significant contribution of GAI in surpassing traditional PdM methods. Notably, GAI excels at addressing limitations caused by a lack of data. This allows for more accurate diagnoses of equipment degradation and the ability to estimate the RUL of aircraft systems with greater precision. The primary objective of utilizing GAI in aircraft PdM is to generate realistic RUL data. This data is then used to predict RUL and diagnose faults. GAI proves particularly adept at tackling challenges like imbalanced and noisy data, information on normal operations significantly where outweighs data on faults. GANs, VAE-GANs, and various combinations of these models are the most prevalent GAI approaches used for predicting aircraft component RUL. the Across reviewed studies, NASA's CMAPSS emerged as the most popular dataset for validating GAI- based PdM models. However, a limitation of this dataset is its focus on turbofan engines. This restricts the possibilities for conducting research on PdM for other aircraft components. 7. References [1] Marais, K. These models were particularly effective in handling non-linear interactions between sensor readings and maintenance outcomes; providing feature importance rankings, allowing for better interpretability and explain- ability in maintenance decision-making; detecting rare but critical fault occurrences; and improving overall predictive accuracy. By integrating correlation-based feature selection with tree classifiers and deep learn- ing techniques, the ATSaaS predictive maintenance model achieved higher fault detection rates, reduced false positives, and improved predictive accuracy. This hybrid approach ensures that maintenance interventions are both data-driven and operationally reliable, minimizing downtime and optimizing resource allocation. 3.6. Results of AI-Based Fault Detection Modeling The modeling and evaluation of the customization-based, personalization-based, and hybrid AI-based fault detection models were conducted to assess their performance across key maintenance parameters: fault detection rate FDR, predictive maintenance effectiveness PME, operational downtime reduction ODR and cost optimization CO. The results highlight the comparative advantages of each approach and demonstrate the hybrid model’s superior performance in predictive maintenance applications. Figure 27 presents a comparative breakdown of performance scores for each model across the four key evaluation criteria. The results indicate significant variations in the effectiveness of different AI-based fault detection approaches. The Customization Model achieved an FDR of 75%, demonstrating its effectiveness in structured, rule-based detection but lacked adaptability to unforeseen conditions. The Personalization Model used machine learning-based predictive analytics, achieving an FDR of 88% and a significantly higher PME of 92%, indicating its strong real-time fault prediction capabilities. The Hybrid Model consistently outperformed both approaches, achieving 95% FDR, 96% PME, 92% ODR, and 90% CO, demonstrating its opti- mal balance between structured rule-based logic and AI-driven predictive analytics. These results confirm that while Customization provides precise control, and Personalization Processes 2025, 13, 666 36 of 54 enhances predictive capabilities, the Hybrid Model delivers the most effective combination of fault classification accuracy and maintenance optimization. Figure 27. Comparative analysis of AI-based fault detection models. The superior performance of the hybrid ATSaaS model was validated using a com- prehensive dataset collected from 86 small and medium-sized airlines, covering a diverse range of operational conditions, aircraft types, and maintenance records. The dataset includes real-time sensor data, historical maintenance logs, and operational performance metrics, ensuring a robust basis for model evaluation. content.abstract_or_snippet: ] proposed a novel approach for diagnosing faults in aviation hydraulic pumps. They addressed the scarcity of fault samples and integrates local wavelet similarity fusion (LWSF) with a CNN in a method called LWSF_CNN. It works by decomposing vibration signals into wavelet packet coefficients (WPCs) which contain characteristic information at specific frequencies. LWSF then modifies the WPCs of faulty samples by referencing normal samples, ensuring the generated samples remain representative of the fault. Finally, the modified WPCs are used to reconstruct new, augmented faulty samples, increasing the diversity and number of samples for improved fault classification. The CNN is then trained on this balanced dataset and is used to diagnose faults in unseen test samples. 3.5 in GAI based Aircraft PdM? RQ-3: Which application domains exist Recent years have seen a surge in innovative methods for PdM in aviation sector, with many researchers employing a combination of data-driven analysis and machine learning techniques. Below is an overview of the various PdM applications within the aircraft industry. Aero-engine and rolling bearings have been identified to be the leading applications in aircraft where GAI has reached a more mature stage. 3.5.1 Aero-Engine. Aircraft engines are complex machinery and a significant expense for airlines, requiring regular maintenance to ensure smooth operation. These engines rely on a network of sensors to monitor their health, including factors like fan inlet temperature, pressure, and rotational speed. Aircraft engine failures can be catastrophic, so ensuring their reliability is crucial [42]. Predicting RUL of an aircraft engine involves analyzing complex multi- variate time series data of sensors collected throughout the engine's lifecycle, which makes it as one of the most challenging tasks in PHM [37]. Zhang et al. [27] describe the three stages of an engine’s life in their paper: healthy, initial degradation, and severe degradation. The healthy stage has no degradation trends and random sensor readings. The initial degradation stage, however, holds the key to identifying impending failure, but data from this stage is scarce [27, 36]. Existing methods that do not differentiate these stages can lead to false alarms or even accidents. Majority of papers have talked about the challenges which are faced in acquiring degradation data of aircraft. Obtaining enough information on complete engine failures is a significant hurdle. It could take years for an engine such as turbofan engine to naturally degrade from its peak performance to a complete breakdown. In practical scenarios, experiencing failure is highly unfavorable as it poses safety risks

content.abstract_or_snippet: the maintenance needs of air- lines with fleets of varying sizes, making them ideal for both small and large operators. Predictive analytics allow issues to be identified and addressed before they escalate, minimizing downtime and improving fleet reliability. Despite its advantages, personalization also has challenges: The effectiveness of personalization relies heavily on advanced technologies and accurate data inputs, making it susceptible to disruptions if technical issues arise. Initial investments in ATSaaS platforms and staff training may pose challenges for resource-constrained airlines. • While personalization dynamically tailors services, it may not fully address the highly specific, predefined needs that customization can accommodate. The last thesis can be commented on further. One of the primary limitations of personalization-based maintenance approaches is their dependence on accurate and un- interrupted data streams from aircraft sensors and operational databases. Inconsistent or erroneous data can lead to false alarms, misclassifications, or delayed maintenance actions, potentially affecting fleet availability and operational efficiency. To mitigate these risks, the following strategies can be implemented: • • • Implementing multi-source data validation techniques, such as cross-referencing sensor readings with historical maintenance logs and operational benchmarks, ensures higher data reliability and accuracy. Additionally, redundant sensor networks and failover mechanisms help maintain data continuity in case of hardware malfunctions. AI-driven anomaly detection algorithms can identify inconsistencies in incoming data, flagging abnormal sensor behavior and preventing incorrect fault predictions. This enhances the robustness of predictive maintenance models by filtering out faulty or misleading data points. Combining machine learning-based predictive analytics with rule-based verification systems can balance the flexibility of AI-driven models with structured decision- making frameworks, reducing the likelihood of false positives and unverified alerts. Processes 2025, 13, 666 41 of 54 By integrating these strategies, personalization-based predictive maintenance mod- els can achieve greater reliability, minimizing risks associated with inaccurate data and operational disruptions. While customization and personalization are distinct, they are not mutually exclu- sive. Integrating these approaches into a hybrid model offers opportunities to use their respective strengths. For large airlines, customization can remain the foundation of long-term mainte- nance strategies, while personalization tools enhance real-time adaptability and predictive capabilities. For SMAs, personalization can serve as the primary approach, with limited customiza- tion applied to address high-priority needs or unique regulatory requirements. The comparison underscores that neither approach is universally superior; their ef- fectiveness depends on the airline’s size, operational complexity, and resource availability. Customization excels in providing control and precision

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: the maintenance needs of air- lines with fleets of varying sizes, making them ideal for both small and large operators. Predictive analytics allow issues to be identified and addressed before they escalate, minimizing downtime and improving fleet reliability. Despite its advantages, personalization also has challenges: The effectiveness of personalization relies heavily on advanced technologies and accurate data inputs, making it susceptible to disruptions if technical issues arise. Initial investments in ATSaaS platforms and staff training may pose challenges for resource-constrained airlines. • While personalization dynamically tailors services, it may not fully address the highly specific, predefined needs that customization can accommodate. The last thesis can be commented on further. One of the primary limitations of personalization-based maintenance approaches is their dependence on accurate and un- interrupted data streams from aircraft sensors and operational databases. Inconsistent or erroneous data can lead to false alarms, misclassifications, or delayed maintenance actions, potentially affecting fleet availability and operational efficiency. To mitigate these risks, the following strategies can be implemented: • • • Implementing multi-source data validation techniques, such as cross-referencing sensor readings with historical maintenance logs and operational benchmarks, ensures higher data reliability and accuracy. Additionally, redundant sensor networks and failover mechanisms help maintain data continuity in case of hardware malfunctions. AI-driven anomaly detection algorithms can identify inconsistencies in incoming data, flagging abnormal sensor behavior and preventing incorrect fault predictions. This enhances the robustness of predictive maintenance models by filtering out faulty or misleading data points. Combining machine learning-based predictive analytics with rule-based verification systems can balance the flexibility of AI-driven models with structured decision- making frameworks, reducing the likelihood of false positives and unverified alerts. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: serves as the system’s core intelligence, whereas the Decision En-gine balances inputs from both the Rule Processor (handling human-defined rules) and AI Analytics (processing automated data). The Conflict Resolution module harmonizes Processes 2025, 13, 666 23 of 54 Service Processing translates the hybrid decisions into actionable plans through a structured workflow: The task Generator creates specific maintenance tasks, the Schedule Optimizer arranges them efficiently, the Resource Allocator assigns necessary resources, and the Implementation Plan provides detailed execution guidelines. The Feedback System ensures continuous improvement through Performance Track- ing, User Feedback, and System Analytics, generating Optimization Suggestions that feed back into both customization and personalization components. The User Interface Layer provides a Configuration Dashboard where maintenance teams can view both Man- ual Settings and AI Recommendations, with a Validation Interface ensuring oversight of all decisions. This hybrid architecture offers several unique advantages: Balanced integration of human expertise and AI capabilities. Conflict resolution between automated and manual inputs. Continuous optimization through comprehensive feedback loops. Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Stakeholder Questionnaire The aircraft maintenance stakeholder questionnaire was developed as a key com- ponent of the study to gain insights into the perspectives, practices, and expectations of various stakeholders involved in aircraft maintenance. This survey aimed to gather data on the adoption and implementation of ATSaaS platforms, with a particular focus on the integration of customization and personalization in maintenance processes. Un- derstanding stakeholder experiences and preferences is critical for identifying challenges, assessing the effectiveness of existing practices, and shaping the future development of ATSaaS platforms. The questionnaire was designed to capture a comprehensive view of the ecosystem by addressing the needs and roles of key stakeholders, including airlines, MRO providers

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: records frequently include errors [Mark Roboff, SkyThread CEO, 2023]. 4. Data Ownership In addition to data availability and quality, there is some question about data ownership. Many OEMs restrict access to operational data for airlines and MROs (Ref. 16, 33). Finally, pilot unions sometimes negotiate limitations on how the data can be used or requirements to anonymize data [Amy O’Dell, United, 2023]. Conversely, it can also be diffi- cult for the Engine OEM to get access to operator’s essential data (e.g. data regarding EHM, flight connections, on-wing maintenance etc.). These data limitations or data anonymizations sometimes make it difficult to use the data for predictive maintenance. Finally, labeled relevant degradation datasets are essential for model building and validation in academia and industry. This can be seen in the impact of existing datasets like the CMAPSS datasets (Ref. 34, 35). 7.2 Investments Needed A few contributions that could help close this barrier are described below. They are clustered into those that aid in accessing more data and those that improve the way data is dealt with. 7.2.1 Accessing more Data 1. Develop New Sensing Technologies New sensing technologies (e.g., embedded or printed sensors or active sensing techniques) can help generate data relevant for prognostics that may not exist now. [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023] 2. Data Export from Aircraft There is ongoing discussion and first approaches among some airlines and airports to explore technologies and methods for exporting data from aircraft (e.g., gate link and satellite) to secure and accessible storage. Further work is needed in order to make use of the data for predictive maintenance operations, model building, and tool validation. [Mark Roboff, SkyThread, 2023][Amy O’Dell, United, 2023][Madhav Misra, RISE, 2023] 3. Digitize Maintenance Records Already underway by many airlines and MROs, mainte- nance records should be standardized, digitized, and parameterised (for machine readability) so they can be available for predictive maintenance model building and validation. [Madhav Misra, RISE, 2023][Marc Roboff, SkyThread, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Deutsch- land, 2023] 4. Develop and Mature Methods and Technologies for Automated Inspection Mature at-gate

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and Internet of Things (IoT) sensor technologies have enabled a shift toward predictive maintenance and condition-based maintenance (CBM) strategies. These methodologies use real-time data collection, intelligent fault classification, and automated control processes to detect early signs of degradation, classify anomalies, and predict failures before they result in costly malfunctions. To fully utilize these capabilities, the Aviation Technical Support as a Service (ATSaaS) platform was proposed [1]. It integrates AI-driven predictive analytics into its core architecture, enabling scalable and adaptive maintenance solutions. By incorporating real-time fault detection and automated decision-making, ATSaaS enhances traditional maintenance frameworks, optimizing operational efficiency and minimizing downtime for both large airlines and smaller operators. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: high- light the need for continued investigation into aviation maintenance optimization. They also underscore the importance of considering practical implementation factors alongside theoretical frameworks when developing maintenance solutions for the aviation industry. 4.9. Future Research Directions The integration of customization and personalization in aircraft maintenance repre- sents a significant shift in how airlines and service providers manage operational efficiency and safety. While the current frameworks of traditional MRO models and ATSaaS platforms have demonstrated their value, further research is needed to explore emerging technolo- gies, address implementation challenges, and unlock new possibilities for innovation in this domain. Advanced predictive analytics represents a critical area for future studies, focusing on enhancing maintenance capabilities through machine learning and artificial intelligence. This includes developing more accurate failure prediction algorithms for complex systems, Processes 2025, 13, 666 50 of 54 integrating environmental and operational data for nuanced predictive models, and inves- tigating hybrid analytics frameworks that combine predictive and prescriptive approaches for optimized maintenance scheduling. Research into the integration of customization and personalization requires deeper exploration, particularly in developing hybrid models that effectively combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization. Key areas include identifying optimal frameworks for integrating manual customization with data-driven personal- ization, assessing operational and economic impacts on airlines of different sizes, and developing methodologies for dynamically transitioning between approaches based on real-time conditions. The scalability of ATSaaS platforms warrants further investigation as these systems gain broader adoption. Research should focus on evaluating how platforms can efficiently scale predictive maintenance capabilities for large fleets without compromising accuracy, in- vestigating cost-effective solutions for small and medium-sized airlines, and exploring mod- ular design approaches that enable seamless adaptation to growing operational demands. Cybersecurity in aircraft maintenance emerges as a crucial research priority given the increasing reliance on digital tools and data-driven platforms. Studies should assess vulnerabilities in ATSaaS platforms and maintenance technologies, investigate blockchain technology’s potential for enhancing data security and traceability, and design robust contingency plans and resilience frameworks to mitigate cyber risks. Economic viability and return on investment analysis require thorough investigation through cost-benefit analyses of different maintenance models, assessment of ROI for airlines transitioning to ATSaaS platforms, and exploration of financial incentives and business models to encourage adoption among smaller operators. Future research in aircraft maintenance should ultimately aim to enhance the inte- gration of advanced technologies, refine hybrid models, and address challenges related to scalability, cybersecurity, and sustainability. These efforts will contribute to developing innovative, efficient, and environmentally responsible maintenance practices that meet the evolving demands of the aviation industry while improving

domain_applicability.reason: Future-Research/Implementierungsaspekte und Personalisierung in Maintenance als generisches Konzept; übertragbar, jedoch nicht konkret zu Halluzination/Traceability.

hypotheses.statement: Der primäre, derzeit in der Literatur zu Aviation-PdM dominierende Beitrag generativer KI liegt in der Generierung/Erweiterung synthetischer Degradations- und Run-to-Failure-Zeitreihendaten (v. a. per GAN/VAE), um Datenknappheit, Imbalance und Rauschen zu adressieren und dadurch Diagnose sowie RUL-Prognose für Flugzeugsysteme zu verbessern.
hypotheses.reasoning: Das SLR beschreibt Datenknappheit durch präventive Wartung (fehlende Run-to-Failure-Daten) als zentrales Hindernis und positioniert Generative AI als Lösung über synthetische Daten; es nennt GANs/VAEs als häufigste Modelle und benennt die Generierung realistischer RUL-/Degradationsdaten als Hauptziel, um Fault Diagnosis und RUL-Schätzung zu verbessern.

hypotheses.statement: Eine praxistaugliche Rolle von (Gen)AI in luftfahrtbezogenen Predictive-Maintenance-Systemen ist die Einbettung in hybride Entscheidungs- und Service-Workflows (Task-Generierung, Scheduling, Ressourcenallokation) mit expliziter Human-in-the-Loop-Validierung und Konfliktauflösung zwischen regelbasierten Vorgaben und datengetriebener KI-Analytik, um Fehlalarme zu reduzieren und regulatorische/sicherheitsrelevante Anforderungen an menschliche Aufsicht zu stützen.
hypotheses.reasoning: Kabashkin beschreibt eine Hybrid-Architektur mit Decision Engine, Rule Processor und AI Analytics, Konfliktauflösung, Feedback-Loops sowie Validation Interface zur Sicherstellung menschlicher Oversight; zudem werden Dateninkonsistenzen als Ursache von False Alarms diskutiert und durch Multi-Source-Validierung plus Kombination aus ML und regelbasierter Verifikation mitigiert.

B. synthetische Degradationsdaten) und in unterstützenden/überwachten Entscheidungsstrukturen; zentrale Implementierungsbarrieren bleiben Datendigitalisierung/-qualität und Zugriff auf Betriebs- und Wartungsdaten.

However, the quantity and quality of the data gathered are crucial to the prediction's accuracy because inaccurate or insufficient data might provide erroneous models [12]. Finally, the hybrid approach combines these methods. The recent surge in machine data, combined with the power of deep learning, is significantly improving the accuracy of PdM models. This leads to more targeted the maintenance resources on strategies, focusing they turn components most likely to fail, ultimately leading to optimized maintenance plans [8]. As industrial demands for efficiency and reliability are pushing traditional maintenance methods to their limits, Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) has emerged as a powerful solution in aviation industry [10], in which PdM is also a key element [12]. By analyzing real-time and historical data, PHM can assess aircraft equipment health and predict future issues. Machine learning, a key tool within PHM, utilizes algorithms trained on vast amounts of data to predict equipment health or remaining useful life (RUL) [6]. However, a major hurdle lies in the need for labeled data (data with known outcomes) for training these algorithms [13]. Limited labeled data can significantly restrict the effectiveness of machine learning in PdM [14]. Acquiring the ideal data for machine learning in PdM for aircraft is practically challenging. This data, commonly known as "run-to-failure" data, consists of time-series readings that capture the gradual degradation of a component – from a healthy state all the way to complete failure [15]. The problem lies in the extended timelines involved. For instance, a seemingly minor issue in a critical aircraft part might take months or even years to progress to complete failure under normal operating conditions. However, allowing a component to reach this point is not only unacceptable but also poses a serious safety risk. To prioritize safety, aircraft parts are routinely replaced well before reaching a critical failure state. Researchers are actively addressing this challenge by developing generative AI models that can predict future equipment health even with scarce failure data [16]. In order to improve PdM procedures in the aviation sector and get beyond data constraints, generative AI (GAI) models are an effective technique. These algorithms may create synthetic data that imitates real-world "run-to-failure" circumstances after being trained on available data, even if it is incomplete. This synthetic data can then be used to train other machine learning algorithms for PdM tasks. Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment

A variety of methods are included in generative AI, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and variational autoencoders (VAEs). VAEs generate unique and credible data points by examining underlying patterns in the data [8]. GANs, on the other hand, employ a collaborative competition between two neural networks. One network generates data, while the other strives to differentiate between real and generated data. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: . The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly analyze papers sourced from four scientific databases. These papers focus on predictive maintenance of aircraft systems through generative AI models. The findings of this review examine various aspects of GAI-powered predictive maintenance, including its objectives, the diversity of models employed, areas of application, datasets used for model validation, and the prevailing challenges and emerging trends. The review identified that most commonly used GAI models in aircraft PdM are GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks), VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) or combination of these models. However, knowledge sharing, model integration and specificity are the key challenges associated with the implementation of these models. This strong knowledge -base will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners aiming to improve their knowledge and implementation of GAI- driven models for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. Keywords: Generative AI, Predictive Maintenance (PdM), Aircraft Maintenance, Generative Adversarial Network, Data Augmentation 1. Introduction Maintenance plays a pivotal role in the aviation industry, with its importance deeply rooted in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft operations. Operating in an environment characterized by extreme temperatures, high altitudes, and various forces during flight, aircraft systems undergo age-related degradation and operational wear without regular maintenance. The repercussions of such neglect are severe, leading to system failures that can compromise the safety of passengers and crew on board [1]. Conversely, the financial ramifications of grounded aircraft due to insufficient maintenance are substantial, compelling airlines to prioritize comprehensive maintenance programs [2]. Regular maintenance actions not only strengthen the structural and operational integrity of aircraft but also extend the lifespan of critical equipment, mitigating revenue losses associated with unplanned downtime [3]. According to a report by Statista, the financial burden of unscheduled aircraft maintenance is anticipated to skyrocket in the aviation industry, reaching an estimated annual cost of nearly 39 billion USD by 2035 [4]. This projection is particularly alarming considering the expected surge in the global fleet size, forecasted to almost double from 25,900 to 47,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems

The research questions themselves might have missed crucial aspects or made assumptions due to the evolving nature of the field and the vast number of approaches for developing these that technologies. Moreover, only considering studies combined GAI with predictive maintenance limited the findings' generalizability. This means they might not be applicable to other forms of predictive maintenance techniques. 6. Conclusion This study explored the growing use of Generative AI in transforming how the aviation industry performs predictive maintenance on aircraft. A systematic literature review was conducted, analyzing studies published over the past five years. The focus was on understanding how GAI is the role revolutionizing aircraft PdM. This generative models play, the specific models being used, the areas where GAI is applied, and the datasets employed to validate these models. included The study implemented a semi-automated approach for the literature review, utilizing ASReview and PRISMA techniques. This process identified 16 relevant studies out of a pool of 442. The findings highlight the significant contribution of GAI in surpassing traditional PdM methods. Notably, GAI excels at addressing limitations caused by a lack of data. This allows for more accurate diagnoses of equipment degradation and the ability to estimate the RUL of aircraft systems with greater precision. The primary objective of utilizing GAI in aircraft PdM is to generate realistic RUL data. This data is then used to predict RUL and diagnose faults. GAI proves particularly adept at tackling challenges like imbalanced and noisy data, information on normal operations significantly where outweighs data on faults. GANs, VAE-GANs, and various combinations of these models are the most prevalent GAI approaches used for predicting aircraft component RUL. the Across reviewed studies, NASA's CMAPSS emerged as the most popular dataset for validating GAI- based PdM models. However, a limitation of this dataset is its focus on turbofan engines. This restricts the possibilities for conducting research on PdM for other aircraft components. 7. References [1] Marais, K. By integrating correlation-based feature selection with tree classifiers and deep learn- ing techniques, the ATSaaS predictive maintenance model achieved higher fault detection rates, reduced false positives, and improved predictive accuracy. This hybrid approach ensures that maintenance interventions are both data-driven and operationally reliable, minimizing downtime and optimizing resource allocation. 3.6. Results of AI-Based Fault Detection Modeling The modeling and evaluation of the customization-based, personalization-based, and hybrid AI-based fault detection models were conducted to assess their performance across key maintenance parameters: fault detection rate FDR, predictive maintenance effectiveness PME, operational downtime reduction ODR and cost optimization CO. The results highlight the comparative advantages of each approach and demonstrate the hybrid model’s superior performance in predictive maintenance applications. Figure 27 presents a comparative breakdown of performance scores for each model across the four key evaluation criteria. The results indicate significant variations in the effectiveness of different AI-based fault detection approaches. The Customization Model achieved an FDR of 75%, demonstrating its effectiveness in structured, rule-based detection but lacked adaptability to unforeseen conditions. The Personalization Model used machine learning-based predictive analytics, achieving an FDR of 88% and a significantly higher PME of 92%, indicating its strong real-time fault prediction capabilities. The Hybrid Model consistently outperformed both approaches, achieving 95% FDR, 96% PME, 92% ODR, and 90% CO, demonstrating its opti- mal balance between structured rule-based logic and AI-driven predictive analytics. These results confirm that while Customization provides precise control, and Personalization Processes 2025, 13, 666 36 of 54 enhances predictive capabilities, the Hybrid Model delivers the most effective combination of fault classification accuracy and maintenance optimization. Figure 27. Comparative analysis of AI-based fault detection models. The superior performance of the hybrid ATSaaS model was validated using a com- prehensive dataset collected from 86 small and medium-sized airlines, covering a diverse range of operational conditions, aircraft types, and maintenance records. The dataset includes real-time sensor data, historical maintenance logs, and operational performance metrics, ensuring a robust basis for model evaluation. content.abstract_or_snippet: ] proposed a novel approach for diagnosing faults in aviation hydraulic pumps. They addressed the scarcity of fault samples and integrates local wavelet similarity fusion (LWSF) with a CNN in a method called LWSF_CNN. It works by decomposing vibration signals into wavelet packet coefficients (WPCs) which contain characteristic information at specific frequencies. LWSF then modifies the WPCs of faulty samples by referencing normal samples, ensuring the generated samples remain representative of the fault. Finally, the modified WPCs are used to reconstruct new, augmented faulty samples, increasing the diversity and number of samples for improved fault classification. The CNN is then trained on this balanced dataset and is used to diagnose faults in unseen test samples. 3.5 in GAI based Aircraft PdM? RQ-3: Which application domains exist Recent years have seen a surge in innovative methods for PdM in aviation sector, with many researchers employing a combination of data-driven analysis and machine learning techniques. Below is an overview of the various PdM applications within the aircraft industry. Aero-engine and rolling bearings have been identified to be the leading applications in aircraft where GAI has reached a more mature stage. 3.5.1 Aero-Engine. Aircraft engines are complex machinery and a significant expense for airlines, requiring regular maintenance to ensure smooth operation. These engines rely on a network of sensors to monitor their health, including factors like fan inlet temperature, pressure, and rotational speed. Aircraft engine failures can be catastrophic, so ensuring their reliability is crucial [42]. Predicting RUL of an aircraft engine involves analyzing complex multi- variate time series data of sensors collected throughout the engine's lifecycle, which makes it as one of the most challenging tasks in PHM [37]. Zhang et al. [27] describe the three stages of an engine’s life in their paper: healthy, initial degradation, and severe degradation. The healthy stage has no degradation trends and random sensor readings. The initial degradation stage, however, holds the key to identifying impending failure, but data from this stage is scarce [27, 36]. Existing methods that do not differentiate these stages can lead to false alarms or even accidents. Majority of papers have talked about the challenges which are faced in acquiring degradation data of aircraft. Obtaining enough information on complete engine failures is a significant hurdle. It could take years for an engine such as turbofan engine to naturally degrade from its peak performance to a complete breakdown. In practical scenarios, experiencing failure is highly unfavorable as it poses safety risks

content.abstract_or_snippet: the maintenance needs of air- lines with fleets of varying sizes, making them ideal for both small and large operators. Predictive analytics allow issues to be identified and addressed before they escalate, minimizing downtime and improving fleet reliability. Despite its advantages, personalization also has challenges: The effectiveness of personalization relies heavily on advanced technologies and accurate data inputs, making it susceptible to disruptions if technical issues arise. Initial investments in ATSaaS platforms and staff training may pose challenges for resource-constrained airlines. • While personalization dynamically tailors services, it may not fully address the highly specific, predefined needs that customization can accommodate. The last thesis can be commented on further. One of the primary limitations of personalization-based maintenance approaches is their dependence on accurate and un- interrupted data streams from aircraft sensors and operational databases. Inconsistent or erroneous data can lead to false alarms, misclassifications, or delayed maintenance actions, potentially affecting fleet availability and operational efficiency. To mitigate these risks, the following strategies can be implemented: • • • Implementing multi-source data validation techniques, such as cross-referencing sensor readings with historical maintenance logs and operational benchmarks, ensures higher data reliability and accuracy. Additionally, redundant sensor networks and failover mechanisms help maintain data continuity in case of hardware malfunctions. AI-driven anomaly detection algorithms can identify inconsistencies in incoming data, flagging abnormal sensor behavior and preventing incorrect fault predictions. This enhances the robustness of predictive maintenance models by filtering out faulty or misleading data points. Combining machine learning-based predictive analytics with rule-based verification systems can balance the flexibility of AI-driven models with structured decision- making frameworks, reducing the likelihood of false positives and unverified alerts. Processes 2025, 13, 666 41 of 54 By integrating these strategies, personalization-based predictive maintenance mod- els can achieve greater reliability, minimizing risks associated with inaccurate data and operational disruptions. While customization and personalization are distinct, they are not mutually exclu- sive. Integrating these approaches into a hybrid model offers opportunities to use their respective strengths. For large airlines, customization can remain the foundation of long-term mainte- nance strategies, while personalization tools enhance real-time adaptability and predictive capabilities. For SMAs, personalization can serve as the primary approach, with limited customiza- tion applied to address high-priority needs or unique regulatory requirements. The comparison underscores that neither approach is universally superior; their ef- fectiveness depends on the airline’s size, operational complexity, and resource availability. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: the maintenance needs of air- lines with fleets of varying sizes, making them ideal for both small and large operators. Predictive analytics allow issues to be identified and addressed before they escalate, minimizing downtime and improving fleet reliability. Despite its advantages, personalization also has challenges: The effectiveness of personalization relies heavily on advanced technologies and accurate data inputs, making it susceptible to disruptions if technical issues arise. Initial investments in ATSaaS platforms and staff training may pose challenges for resource-constrained airlines. • While personalization dynamically tailors services, it may not fully address the highly specific, predefined needs that customization can accommodate. The last thesis can be commented on further. One of the primary limitations of personalization-based maintenance approaches is their dependence on accurate and un- interrupted data streams from aircraft sensors and operational databases. Inconsistent or erroneous data can lead to false alarms, misclassifications, or delayed maintenance actions, potentially affecting fleet availability and operational efficiency. To mitigate these risks, the following strategies can be implemented: • • • Implementing multi-source data validation techniques, such as cross-referencing sensor readings with historical maintenance logs and operational benchmarks, ensures higher data reliability and accuracy. Additionally, redundant sensor networks and failover mechanisms help maintain data continuity in case of hardware malfunctions. AI-driven anomaly detection algorithms can identify inconsistencies in incoming data, flagging abnormal sensor behavior and preventing incorrect fault predictions. This enhances the robustness of predictive maintenance models by filtering out faulty or misleading data points. Combining machine learning-based predictive analytics with rule-based verification systems can balance the flexibility of AI-driven models with structured decision- making frameworks, reducing the likelihood of false positives and unverified alerts. Processes 2025, 13, 666 41 of 54 By integrating these strategies, personalization-based predictive maintenance mod- els can achieve greater reliability, minimizing risks associated with inaccurate data and operational disruptions. While customization and personalization are distinct, they are not mutually exclu- sive. Integrating these approaches into a hybrid model offers opportunities to use their respective strengths. For large airlines, customization can remain the foundation of long-term mainte- nance strategies, while personalization tools enhance real-time adaptability and predictive capabilities. For SMAs, personalization can serve as the primary approach, with limited customiza- tion applied to address high-priority needs or unique regulatory requirements. The comparison underscores that neither approach is universally superior; their ef- fectiveness depends on the airline’s size, operational complexity, and resource availability. Customization excels in providing control and precision
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems.pdf

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: serves as the system’s core intelligence, whereas the Decision En-gine balances inputs from both the Rule Processor (handling human-defined rules) and AI Analytics (processing automated data). The Conflict Resolution module harmonizes Processes 2025, 13, 666 23 of 54 Service Processing translates the hybrid decisions into actionable plans through a structured workflow: The task Generator creates specific maintenance tasks, the Schedule Optimizer arranges them efficiently, the Resource Allocator assigns necessary resources, and the Implementation Plan provides detailed execution guidelines. The Feedback System ensures continuous improvement through Performance Track- ing, User Feedback, and System Analytics, generating Optimization Suggestions that feed back into both customization and personalization components. The User Interface Layer provides a Configuration Dashboard where maintenance teams can view both Man- ual Settings and AI Recommendations, with a Validation Interface ensuring oversight of all decisions. This hybrid architecture offers several unique advantages: Balanced integration of human expertise and AI capabilities. Conflict resolution between automated and manual inputs. Continuous optimization through comprehensive feedback loops. Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Stakeholder Questionnaire The aircraft maintenance stakeholder questionnaire was developed as a key com- ponent of the study to gain insights into the perspectives, practices, and expectations of various stakeholders involved in aircraft maintenance. This survey aimed to gather data on the adoption and implementation of ATSaaS platforms, with a particular focus on the integration of customization and personalization in maintenance processes. Un- derstanding stakeholder experiences and preferences is critical for identifying challenges, assessing the effectiveness of existing practices, and shaping the future development of ATSaaS platforms. The questionnaire was designed to capture a comprehensive view of the ecosystem by addressing the needs and roles of key stakeholders, including airlines, MRO providers

content.abstract_or_snippet: serves as the system’s core intelligence, whereas the Decision En-gine balances inputs from both the Rule Processor (handling human-defined rules) and AI Analytics (processing automated data). The Conflict Resolution module harmonizes Processes 2025, 13, 666 23 of 54 Service Processing translates the hybrid decisions into actionable plans through a structured workflow: The task Generator creates specific maintenance tasks, the Schedule Optimizer arranges them efficiently, the Resource Allocator assigns necessary resources, and the Implementation Plan provides detailed execution guidelines. The Feedback System ensures continuous improvement through Performance Track- ing, User Feedback, and System Analytics, generating Optimization Suggestions that feed back into both customization and personalization components. The User Interface Layer provides a Configuration Dashboard where maintenance teams can view both Man- ual Settings and AI Recommendations, with a Validation Interface ensuring oversight of all decisions. This hybrid architecture offers several unique advantages: Balanced integration of human expertise and AI capabilities. Conflict resolution between automated and manual inputs. Continuous optimization through comprehensive feedback loops. Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Stakeholder Questionnaire The aircraft maintenance stakeholder questionnaire was developed as a key com- ponent of the study to gain insights into the perspectives, practices, and expectations of various stakeholders involved in aircraft maintenance. This survey aimed to gather data on the adoption and implementation of ATSaaS platforms, with a particular focus on the integration of customization and personalization in maintenance processes. Un- derstanding stakeholder experiences and preferences is critical for identifying challenges, assessing the effectiveness of existing practices, and shaping the future development of ATSaaS platforms. The questionnaire was designed to capture a comprehensive view of the ecosystem by addressing the needs and roles of key stakeholders, including airlines, MRO providers

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: records frequently include errors [Mark Roboff, SkyThread CEO, 2023]. 4. Data Ownership In addition to data availability and quality, there is some question about data ownership. Many OEMs restrict access to operational data for airlines and MROs (Ref. 16, 33). Finally, pilot unions sometimes negotiate limitations on how the data can be used or requirements to anonymize data [Amy O’Dell, United, 2023]. Conversely, it can also be diffi- cult for the Engine OEM to get access to operator’s essential data (e.g. data regarding EHM, flight connections, on-wing maintenance etc.). These data limitations or data anonymizations sometimes make it difficult to use the data for predictive maintenance. Finally, labeled relevant degradation datasets are essential for model building and validation in academia and industry. This can be seen in the impact of existing datasets like the CMAPSS datasets (Ref. 34, 35). 7.2 Investments Needed A few contributions that could help close this barrier are described below. They are clustered into those that aid in accessing more data and those that improve the way data is dealt with. 7.2.1 Accessing more Data 1. Develop New Sensing Technologies New sensing technologies (e.g., embedded or printed sensors or active sensing techniques) can help generate data relevant for prognostics that may not exist now. [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023] 2. Data Export from Aircraft There is ongoing discussion and first approaches among some airlines and airports to explore technologies and methods for exporting data from aircraft (e.g., gate link and satellite) to secure and accessible storage. Further work is needed in order to make use of the data for predictive maintenance operations, model building, and tool validation. [Mark Roboff, SkyThread, 2023][Amy O’Dell, United, 2023][Madhav Misra, RISE, 2023] 3. Digitize Maintenance Records Already underway by many airlines and MROs, mainte- nance records should be standardized, digitized, and parameterised (for machine readability) so they can be available for predictive maintenance model building and validation. [Madhav Misra, RISE, 2023][Marc Roboff, SkyThread, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Deutsch- land, 2023] 4. Develop and Mature Methods and Technologies for Automated Inspection Mature at-gate

The growing complexity of mod- ern aviation systems necessitates the integration of advanced fault detection and control mechanisms to optimize maintenance processes and enhance operational resilience. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and Internet of Things (IoT) sensor technologies have enabled a shift toward predictive maintenance and condition-based maintenance (CBM) strategies. These methodologies use real-time data collection, intelligent fault classification, and automated control processes to detect early signs of degradation, classify anomalies, and predict failures before they result in costly malfunctions. To fully utilize these capabilities, the Aviation Technical Support as a Service (ATSaaS) platform was proposed [1]. It integrates AI-driven predictive analytics into its core architecture, enabling scalable and adaptive maintenance solutions. By incorporating real-time fault detection and automated decision-making, ATSaaS enhances traditional maintenance frameworks, optimizing operational efficiency and minimizing downtime for both large airlines and smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: high- light the need for continued investigation into aviation maintenance optimization. They also underscore the importance of considering practical implementation factors alongside theoretical frameworks when developing maintenance solutions for the aviation industry. 4.9. Future Research Directions The integration of customization and personalization in aircraft maintenance repre- sents a significant shift in how airlines and service providers manage operational efficiency and safety. While the current frameworks of traditional MRO models and ATSaaS platforms have demonstrated their value, further research is needed to explore emerging technolo- gies, address implementation challenges, and unlock new possibilities for innovation in this domain. Advanced predictive analytics represents a critical area for future studies, focusing on enhancing maintenance capabilities through machine learning and artificial intelligence. This includes developing more accurate failure prediction algorithms for complex systems, Processes 2025, 13, 666 50 of 54 integrating environmental and operational data for nuanced predictive models, and inves- tigating hybrid analytics frameworks that combine predictive and prescriptive approaches for optimized maintenance scheduling. Research into the integration of customization and personalization requires deeper exploration, particularly in developing hybrid models that effectively combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization. Key areas include identifying optimal frameworks for integrating manual customization with data-driven personal- ization, assessing operational and economic impacts on airlines of different sizes, and developing methodologies for dynamically transitioning between approaches based on real-time conditions. The scalability of ATSaaS platforms warrants further investigation as these systems gain broader adoption. Research should focus on evaluating how platforms can efficiently scale predictive maintenance capabilities for large fleets without compromising accuracy, in- vestigating cost-effective solutions for small and medium-sized airlines, and exploring mod- ular design approaches that enable seamless adaptation to growing operational demands. Cybersecurity in aircraft maintenance emerges as a crucial research priority given the increasing reliance on digital tools and data-driven platforms. Studies should assess vulnerabilities in ATSaaS platforms and maintenance technologies, investigate blockchain technology’s potential for enhancing data security and traceability, and design robust contingency plans and resilience frameworks to mitigate cyber risks. Economic viability and return on investment analysis require thorough investigation through cost-benefit analyses of different maintenance models, assessment of ROI for airlines transitioning to ATSaaS platforms, and exploration of financial incentives and business models to encourage adoption among smaller operators. Future research in aircraft maintenance should ultimately aim to enhance the inte- gration of advanced technologies, refine hybrid models, and address challenges related to scalability, cybersecurity, and sustainability. These efforts will contribute to developing innovative, efficient, and environmentally responsible maintenance practices that meet the evolving demands of the aviation industry while improving

Allerdings wird „generative“ KI dabei nicht gezeigt; es geht allgemein um AI/ML in ATSaaS. Daher bleibt die GenAI-spezifische Praxistauglichkeit durch diese Evidenz nur indirekt gestützt.

updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Evidenz stützt beide Teile teilweise: (a) SLR positioniert GenAI klar als Mittel zur Verbesserung der Datenbasis via synthetische RUL/Degradationsdaten (kurzfristig plausibler Hebel) und (b) Kabashkin stützt die Rolle unterstützender/überwachter Entscheidungsstrukturen (Validation Interface, Human Oversight) – allerdings nicht spezifisch für GenAI. NASA-TM belegt starke Implementierungsbarrieren (Datenqualität, Fehler in Records, Data Ownership/Access, Bedarf an mehr/standardisierten Daten). Die relative Aussage „größte Hebelwirkung … weniger autonom“ bleibt mangels direkter Gegenüberstellung in den Dokumenten nicht abschließend validiert.

Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation: A Systematic Literature Review Zeeshan Ullah Khan a, Bisma Nasim a, Zeehasham Rasheed b* Zeeshan Ullah Khan: Conceptualization (equal); Methodology (equal); writing – original draft (lead), Bisma Nasim: Conceptualization (equal); Methodology (equal); writing – review and editing (equal); formal analysis (lead), Zeehasham Rasheed: Writing – review and editing (equal); Conceptualization (supporting) a) Department of IT & Technology, IU Internationale Hochschule, Juri-Gagarin-Ring 152, 99084 Erfurt, Germany b) Department of Information Sciences and Technology, George Mason University, Fairfax VA, USA *Corresponding author: zrasheed@gmu.edu ORCID: 0000-0003-4394-2957 Funding: None Conflict of interest statement: The authors have declared no conflict of interest. Permission to reproduce materials from other sources: None Data Availability statement: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author, MV , upon reasonable request. Abstract Predictive maintenance (PdM) is a critical tool in aviation, promoting sustainability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. However, a significant challenge in implementing predictive maintenance framework is limited run-to-failure data due to frequent preventive maintenance. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly

Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. The research questions themselves might have missed crucial aspects or made assumptions due to the evolving nature of the field and the vast number of approaches for developing these that technologies. Moreover, only considering studies combined GAI with predictive maintenance limited the findings' generalizability. This means they might not be applicable to other forms of predictive maintenance techniques. 6. Conclusion This study explored the growing use of Generative AI in transforming how the aviation industry performs predictive maintenance on aircraft. A systematic literature review was conducted, analyzing studies published over the past five years. The focus was on understanding how GAI is the role revolutionizing aircraft PdM. This generative models play, the specific models being used, the areas where GAI is applied, and the datasets employed to validate these models. included The study implemented a semi-automated approach for the literature review, utilizing ASReview and PRISMA techniques. This process identified 16 relevant studies out of a pool of 442. The findings highlight the significant contribution of GAI in surpassing traditional PdM methods. Notably, GAI excels at addressing limitations caused by a lack of data. This allows for more accurate diagnoses of equipment degradation and the ability to estimate the RUL of aircraft systems with greater precision. The primary objective of utilizing GAI in aircraft PdM is to generate realistic RUL data. This data is then used to predict RUL and diagnose faults. GAI proves particularly adept at tackling challenges like imbalanced and noisy data, information on normal operations significantly where outweighs data on faults. GANs, VAE-GANs, and various combinations of these models are the most prevalent GAI approaches used for predicting aircraft component RUL. the Across reviewed studies, NASA's CMAPSS emerged as the most popular dataset for validating GAI- based PdM models. However, a limitation of this dataset is its focus on turbofan engines. This restricts the possibilities for conducting research on PdM for other aircraft components. 7. References [1] Marais, K. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: . The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly analyze papers sourced from four scientific databases. These papers focus on predictive maintenance of aircraft systems through generative AI models. The findings of this review examine various aspects of GAI-powered predictive maintenance, including its objectives, the diversity of models employed, areas of application, datasets used for model validation, and the prevailing challenges and emerging trends. The review identified that most commonly used GAI models in aircraft PdM are GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks), VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) or combination of these models. However, knowledge sharing, model integration and specificity are the key challenges associated with the implementation of these models. This strong knowledge -base will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners aiming to improve their knowledge and implementation of GAI- driven models for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. Keywords: Generative AI, Predictive Maintenance (PdM), Aircraft Maintenance, Generative Adversarial Network, Data Augmentation 1. Introduction Maintenance plays a pivotal role in the aviation industry, with its importance deeply rooted in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft operations. Operating in an environment characterized by extreme temperatures, high altitudes, and various forces during flight, aircraft systems undergo age-related degradation and operational wear without regular maintenance. The repercussions of such neglect are severe, leading to system failures that can compromise the safety of passengers and crew on board [1]. Conversely, the financial ramifications of grounded aircraft due to insufficient maintenance are substantial, compelling airlines to prioritize comprehensive maintenance programs [2]. Regular maintenance actions not only strengthen the structural and operational integrity of aircraft but also extend the lifespan of critical equipment, mitigating revenue losses associated with unplanned downtime [3]. According to a report by Statista, the financial burden of unscheduled aircraft maintenance is anticipated to skyrocket in the aviation industry, reaching an estimated annual cost of nearly 39 billion USD by 2035 [4]. This projection is particularly alarming considering the expected surge in the global fleet size, forecasted to almost double from 25,900 to 47,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems
