Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment health and RUL more accurately with more extensive training data. A variety of methods are included in generative AI, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and variational autoencoders (VAEs). VAEs generate unique and credible data points by examining underlying patterns in the data [8]. GANs, on the other hand, employ a collaborative competition between two neural networks. One network generates data, while the other strives to differentiate between real and generated data. These adversarial dynamic improvement of both networks, drives ultimately leading to the generation of increasingly realistic synthetic data [13]. the continual 1.1 Research Gap and Related Work Over the past decade, several reviews have explored industries predictive maintenance applications across through various approaches. For instance, Ran et al. [14] and Zhang et al. [12] examined fault diagnosis, prognosis, and machine/deep learning techniques in PdM for diverse industrial settings. While these reviews offered valuable insights, they lacked a specific focus on the aircraft industry. Similarly, Carvalho et al. [15] provided an extensive review of various machine learning methods and datasets for PdM applications, specific equipment. However, their review, like the others, did not explore applications specific to the aviation industry. highlighting approaches for Although, some reviews addressed aircraft applications, but lacked specifics regarding generative AI models for predictive maintenance. Fu et al. [10] reviewed data-driven prognostic algorithms, including conventional and deep learning models. They highlighted aircraft as a key PdM application and reviewed papers using different models for various aircraft equipment. However, their focus was not on generative AI. Similarly, Serradilla et al. [8] reviewed deep learning architectures for PdM stages like anomaly detection and RUL estimation, using aircraft systems as examples. Although they discussed generative models in general but lacked details pertaining to their applications in aircraft PdM. Adryan & Sastra [6] reviewed machine learning for aircraft engine PdM, concluding that LSTM and GRU models deliver high accuracy (over 99%). Abdulrahman et al. content.abstract_or_snippet: metrics like accuracy. To address this challenge, various techniques are being used to balance the data before training the models. This involved oversampling the rare "failure" data or under sampling the abundant "normal" data. Wu et al. [30], Chen et al. [31], Chen et al. [28], Liu et al. [32], Xiong et al. [33] and Fu et al. [34] addressed class imbalance in datasets using generative models. They improved classification accuracy in fault diagnosis and yielded a more balanced dataset by creating synthetic fault data for training diagnostic models. Similarly, Zhang et al. [35], Lv et al. [36], Lang et al. [37], Xiong et al. [33] and Zhang et al. [28] handled the sparse degradation data by creating synthetic time-series data representing different degradation patterns. This data augmentation enriched training datasets for machine learning models, allowing them to identify these early warning signs and predict RUL more accurately. In some cases, sensor data might also be incomplete, with missing readings. Ma et al. [29] proposed that generative models can reconstruct missing entries for time-series data with missing values from multiple sensors, leading to improved downstream tasks like RUL prediction in aeroengines. Furthermore, Huang et al. [38] used variational auto encoders to improve the feature extraction process. They stated that AI models can go beyond simple data augmentation by uncovering hidden patterns within sensor data and generated health index curve, leading to better RUL prediction accuracy. 3.4 used in Aircraft PdM? Different GAI models are best suited for addressing specific data challenges. Researchers are actively exploring ways to make GAI models more interpretable, allowing for a better understanding of how these models arrive at their decisions [39]. The types of Generative AI models commonly implied in the reviewed papers are GANs and VAEs. Table 7 lists the type of generative models identified in the SLR. content.abstract_or_snippet: U models deliver high accuracy (over 99%). Abdulrahman et al. [17] explored deep learning models for detecting aero-engine blade defects, identifying CNN and YOLO as popular choices. They also highlighted challenges like data scarcity and the inability to detect new defects, but generative AI was not the focus. Finally, Wen et al. [18] reviewed data-driven prognostic algorithms, similar to Fu et al. [10], with a focus on aircraft applications. However, generative AI models were not addressed. like medicine and The closest study to generative AI applications in PdM comes from Sabuhi et al. [13]. They explored various security, demonstrating domains generative AI's potential. However, their work lacked an aviation industry focus, highlighting the gap in research on generative AI for predictive maintenance in the aviation sector. In essence, existing systematic reviews offer valuable insights into PdM using various techniques, but they lack a dedicated focus on generative AI models within the specific context of aviation predictive maintenance. This gap presents a compelling opportunity for further research to explore the potential and unique functionalities of generative AI in enhancing predictive maintenance practices for the aviation industry. Keeping in view the above stated facts, to the best of author’s knowledge, this study stands out from previous research in two ways. First, it strictly followed a well- established method called SLR protocol. Second, it narrowed the focus to the specific combination of generative AI and predictive maintenance within the aviation industry. By focusing on a specific niche and employing a systematic approach, this study delivers unique and valuable insights. 1.2 Paper Organization This paper offers a comprehensive review of GAI based PdM in aviation industry, following a structured approach. Chapter 2 outlines the SLR protocol in detail, which combines both manual and semi-automated approaches. Chapter 3 describes the findings of the SLR. It begins by presenting key statistics of the SLR sample, followed by comprehensive exploration of each research question. Chapter 4 dives into a discussion. Subsequently, in chapter 6 the significance of this research is presented and concluding remarks are summarized. 2. In this context, several key factors were considered when finalizing search string for this SLR. A thorough initial search of keywords was initiated with broader terms like "generative artificial intelligence" and "predictive maintenance" in the aviation domain. For instance, the search term "generative artificial intelligence" included studies on various deep learning and machine learning models, which wasn’t relevant to the specific research interest of thesis centered on generative AI models used for predicting aircraft maintenance needs. To address this challenge, a keyword refinement process was carried out. A preliminary review of existing literature was conducted, carefully examining the titles, abstracts, and keywords of relevant articles. This analysis helped identify synonyms, abbreviations, and related terms that could be incorporated into the search string. A crucial metric of predictive maintenance, "Remaining Useful Life," was added. Additionally, interchangeable terms related to Prognostics and Health Management, and "prognostics," were also added. Terms related to generative AI such as "generative," "adversarial," "GAN," "VAE," and "variational autoencoder" were included. All the proposed approaches, combining under-sampling, feature space synthetic data generation, aimed to create a balanced training set with superior synthetic anomalies, ultimately enhancing anomaly detection accuracy in aircraft equipment. Nevertheless, few challenges remain in implementation of GAI models. One of the challenges identified is optimization of the balance between generator and discriminator for realistic synthetic data [30,40]. The current research heavily focuses on sensor data, ignoring the fact that textual and visual data can also augment GAI models. Furthermore, lack of interpretability causes lack of trust in the output of GAI models especially for safety critical systems, as a lesser understanding generally exists into how these models arrive at their conclusion. Also, GAI models trained on specific aircraft system might not work well with other systems, due to sensor data variations. Finally, the implementation of generative models in PdM of aircraft is affected by high-dimensional aircraft data, expensive real-time integration challenges. The successful integration of GAI model in PdM framework depends upon the knowledge dissemination. The lack of knowledge sharing may result in a breakdown in communication and make it difficult to incorporate generative AI models into current operations [27,30,35]. infrastructure, and turbofan engine It was observed that public repositories offer a wealth of data, with the C-MAPSS emerging as the dominant choice for [36,38]. Despite these limitations, there exists positive prospects for research in this field. However, the quantity and quality of the data gathered are crucial to the prediction's accuracy because inaccurate or insufficient data might provide erroneous models [12]. Finally, the hybrid approach combines these methods. The recent surge in machine data, combined with the power of deep learning, is significantly improving the accuracy of PdM models. This leads to more targeted the maintenance resources on strategies, focusing they turn components most likely to fail, ultimately leading to optimized maintenance plans [8]. As industrial demands for efficiency and reliability are pushing traditional maintenance methods to their limits, Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) has emerged as a powerful solution in aviation industry [10], in which PdM is also a key element [12]. By analyzing real-time and historical data, PHM can assess aircraft equipment health and predict future issues. Machine learning, a key tool within PHM, utilizes algorithms trained on vast amounts of data to predict equipment health or remaining useful life (RUL) [6]. However, a major hurdle lies in the need for labeled data (data with known outcomes) for training these algorithms [13]. Limited labeled data can significantly restrict the effectiveness of machine learning in PdM [14]. Acquiring the ideal data for machine learning in PdM for aircraft is practically challenging. This data, commonly known as "run-to-failure" data, consists of time-series readings that capture the gradual degradation of a component – from a healthy state all the way to complete failure [15]. The problem lies in the extended timelines involved. For instance, a seemingly minor issue in a critical aircraft part might take months or even years to progress to complete failure under normal operating conditions. However, allowing a component to reach this point is not only unacceptable but also poses a serious safety risk. To prioritize safety, aircraft parts are routinely replaced well before reaching a critical failure state. Researchers are actively addressing this challenge by developing generative AI models that can predict future equipment health even with scarce failure data [16]. In order to improve PdM procedures in the aviation sector and get beyond data constraints, generative AI (GAI) models are an effective technique. These algorithms may create synthetic data that imitates real-world "run-to-failure" circumstances after being trained on available data, even if it is incomplete. This synthetic data can then be used to train other machine learning algorithms for PdM tasks. Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment

Phrasing search queries can be tricky, and even minor wording changes can significantly alter search results in different databases. Furthermore, the search of relevant studies was concluded in Feb 2024, excluding any research published afterwards. This is a challenge in a rapidly developing field like generative AI. With so many new publications, capturing all relevant literature up to the review's submission date is difficult. Even with efficient research methods, there's always a time gap between data collection and the final review. This means there might be recently published techniques that address the issues identified in this review, but they weren't included due to the cutoff date. This can potentially lead to missing important research on the topic. Another limitation is publication bias. Studies with positive results are more likely to be published than those with negative findings. The authors tried to address this by focusing on high-quality research using specific methods, but there's still a chance relevant information was missed. Selection bias is also a concern. Even with predetermined criteria for including studies, subjective judgments can still influence which ones the research area, are chosen. The broad scope of encompassing both predictive maintenance and generative AI, requires expertise in various fields. This increases the possibility of misinterpreting some studies during the selection process. The data extraction and synthesis process were equally shared between the authors to capture important information needed to answer the research questions. The disagreements were carefully reflected upon, and decisions were made accordingly. Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. This signifies that the active learning method identifies relevant studies at a significantly faster rate. 3.3 RQ-1: What is the role of Generative AI in Aircraft PdM? Aircraft rely on healthy systems for safe and efficient operations, and it is identified that predictive maintenance based on GAI plays a crucial role in capturing the faults before they even occur. Generative AI offers a powerful suite of techniques to address the challenges of imbalanced and scarce data for capturing the early stages of aircraft system degradation. Hence, the primary roles of generative AI are s d r o c e r t n a v e e r l f o r e b m u N 55 46 37 28 18 9 0 1 Active Learning Manual Learning 25 49 74 98 122 Number of reviewed records Fig. 8 Active Learning Vs Manual Learning (1) data augmentation, (2) feature extraction and (3) missing data imputation. Data augmentation refers to generation of new data and augmenting it with existing data. The goal of data augmentation is to increase data quantity and diversity [27]. Moreover, GAI can be used to extract key features of the data for building classifiers and predictive models, hence assisting in learning data representation [28]. Finally, data imputation techniques involve filling in the blanks in the data. Unlike traditional estimation techniques of statistical analysis, neural network methods work in two stages. First, they train a model on the data. Then, in the imputation stage, the trained model is used to "fill in the blanks" for missing values by generating data that follows those learned patterns [29]. Aircraft generate a lot of data through sensors installed but the key problem in implementing PdM is that the data is imbalanced. This means that normal operation readings significantly outnumber anomaly data. This imbalance mirrors situations where the number of healthy samples vastly outweighs defective ones, or the training data fails to represent all fault categories equally. Consequently, GAI models can become biased towards the over-represented normal class, leading to misleading performance metrics like accuracy. To address this challenge, various techniques are being used to balance the data before training the models. This involved oversampling the rare "failure" data or under sampling the abundant "normal" data. Wu et al. [30],

Abstract Predictive maintenance (PdM) is a critical tool in aviation, promoting sustainability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. However, a significant challenge in implementing predictive maintenance framework is limited run-to-failure data due to frequent preventive maintenance. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly

The Feedback System ensures continuous improvement through Performance Track- ing, User Feedback, and System Analytics, generating Optimization Suggestions that feed back into both customization and personalization components. The User Interface Layer provides a Configuration Dashboard where maintenance teams can view both Man- ual Settings and AI Recommendations, with a Validation Interface ensuring oversight of all decisions. This hybrid architecture offers several unique advantages: Balanced integration of human expertise and AI capabilities. Conflict resolution between automated and manual inputs. Continuous optimization through comprehensive feedback loops. Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. The Conflict Resolution module harmonizes Processes 2025, 13, 666 23 of 54 Service Processing translates the hybrid decisions into actionable plans through a structured workflow: The task Generator creates specific maintenance tasks, the Schedule Optimizer arranges them efficiently, the Resource Allocator assigns necessary resources, and the Implementation Plan provides detailed execution guidelines. The Feedback System ensures continuous improvement through Performance Track- ing, User Feedback, and System Analytics, generating Optimization Suggestions that feed back into both customization and personalization components. The User Interface Layer provides a Configuration Dashboard where maintenance teams can view both Man- ual Settings and AI Recommendations, with a Validation Interface ensuring oversight of all decisions. This hybrid architecture offers several unique advantages: Balanced integration of human expertise and AI capabilities. Conflict resolution between automated and manual inputs. Continuous optimization through comprehensive feedback loops. Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Stakeholder Questionnaire The aircraft maintenance stakeholder questionnaire was developed as a key com- ponent of the study to gain insights into the perspectives, practices, and expectations of various stakeholders involved in aircraft maintenance. This survey aimed to gather data on the adoption and implementation of ATSaaS platforms, with a particular focus on the integration of customization and personalization in maintenance processes. Un- derstanding stakeholder experiences and preferences is critical for identifying challenges, assessing the effectiveness of existing practices, and shaping the future development of ATSaaS platforms. The questionnaire was designed to capture a comprehensive view of the ecosystem by addressing the needs and roles of key stakeholders, including airlines, MRO providers

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and Internet of Things (IoT) sensor technologies have enabled a shift toward predictive maintenance and condition-based maintenance (CBM) strategies. These methodologies use real-time data collection, intelligent fault classification, and automated control processes to detect early signs of degradation, classify anomalies, and predict failures before they result in costly malfunctions. To fully utilize these capabilities, the Aviation Technical Support as a Service (ATSaaS) platform was proposed [1]. It integrates AI-driven predictive analytics into its core architecture, enabling scalable and adaptive maintenance solutions. content.abstract_or_snippet: maintenance support ap-proaches. This flexible pricing structure allows airlines to optimize their maintenance costs while ensuring access to high-quality technical support services when needed. 2.4. Aircraft Maintenance and Support Services for Airlines The evolution of digital marketplaces in aviation maintenance is transforming how airlines access technical support, predictive analytics, and MRO services. The ATSaaS model facilitates this shift by offering on-demand, AI-driven maintenance solutions through cloud-based platforms, allowing airlines to optimize operations, reduce costs, and improve fleet reliability. By integrating customization and personalization, ATSaaS enables airlines to select tailored maintenance packages while benefiting from AI-driven fault detection, remote diagnostics, and predictive maintenance strategies. ATSaaS improves fault detection capabilities compared to conventional maintenance methods, which often rely on scheduled inspections and reactive repairs. Unlike tradi-tional time-based or condition-based maintenance approaches, ATSaaS uses real-time sensor data, AI-driven anomaly detection, and predictive analytics to identify potential failures before they escalate into critical malfunctions. Key advantages of ATSaaS over conventional methods include • ATSaaS collects and analyzes real-time data from aircraft sensors, enabling instant fault detection and response. • Machine learning models identify subtle anomalies, and degradation patterns that human technicians or rule-based systems may overlook, allowing earlier and more accurate failure predictions. Processes 2025, 13, 666 11 of 54 ATSaaS improves fault detection capabilities compared to conventional maintenance methods, which often rely on scheduled inspections and reactive repairs. Unlike traditional time-based or condition-based maintenance approaches, ATSaaS uses real-time sensor data, AI-driven anomaly detection, and predictive analytics to identify potential failures before they escalate into critical malfunctions. Key advantages of ATSaaS over conventional methods include • ATSaaS collects and analyzes real-time data from aircraft sensors, enabling instant fault detection and response. • • Machine learning models identify subtle anomalies, and degradation patterns that human technicians or rule-based systems may overlook, allowing earlier and more accurate failure predictions. ATSaaS integrates predictive maintenance recommendations directly into airline main- tenance workflows, reducing the reliance on reactive troubleshooting and minimizing aircraft downtime. Unlike conventional systems that require manual customization, ATSaaS dynamically adjusts to fleet-wide operational conditions, optimizing maintenance schedules for both large airlines and small operators. • By integrating data-driven fault detection with AI-powered automation, ATSaaS enhances maintenance efficiency, reduces operational disruptions, and lowers overall costs, making it a superior alternative to conventional fault detection methods. This flexible pricing structure allows airlines to optimize their maintenance costs while ensuring access to high-quality technical support services when needed. 2.4. Aircraft Maintenance and Support Services for Airlines The evolution of digital marketplaces in aviation maintenance is transforming how airlines access technical support, predictive analytics, and MRO services. The ATSaaS model facilitates this shift by offering on-demand, AI-driven maintenance solutions through cloud-based platforms, allowing airlines to optimize operations, reduce costs, and improve fleet reliability. By integrating customization and personalization, ATSaaS enables airlines to select tailored maintenance packages while benefiting from AI-driven fault detection, remote diagnostics, and predictive maintenance strategies. ATSaaS improves fault detection capabilities compared to conventional maintenance methods, which often rely on scheduled inspections and reactive repairs. Unlike tradi-tional time-based or condition-based maintenance approaches, ATSaaS uses real-time sensor data, AI-driven anomaly detection, and predictive analytics to identify potential failures before they escalate into critical malfunctions. Key advantages of ATSaaS over conventional methods include • ATSaaS collects and analyzes real-time data from aircraft sensors, enabling instant fault detection and response. • Machine learning models identify subtle anomalies, and degradation patterns that human technicians or rule-based systems may overlook, allowing earlier and more accurate failure predictions. ATSaaS integrates predictive maintenance recommendations directly into airline main- tenance workflows, reducing the reliance on reactive troubleshooting and minimizing aircraft downtime. Unlike conventional systems that require manual customization, ATSaaS dynamically adjusts to fleet-wide operational conditions, optimizing maintenance schedules for both large airlines and small operators. • By integrating data-driven fault detection with AI-powered automation, ATSaaS enhances maintenance efficiency, reduces operational disruptions, and lowers overall costs, making it a superior alternative to conventional fault detection methods. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: Article AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems Igor Kabashkin 1,* , Vladimir Perekrestov 2 and Maksim Pivovar 2 1 2 Engineering Faculty, Transport and Telecommunication Institute, Lauvas iela 2, LV-1019 Riga, Latvia Sky Net Technics, Business Center 03, Ras Al-Khaimah B04-223, United Arab Emirates * Correspondence: kiv@tsi.lv Abstract: This study investigates the integration of customization and personalization ap- proaches in aviation maintenance through Aviation Technical Support as a Service (ATSaaS) platform. Through a comprehensive survey of 86 small and medium-sized airlines, com- bined with mathematical modeling of fault detection systems, the study develops and validates a hybrid framework that integrates traditional maintenance approaches with AI-driven solutions. The comparative analysis demonstrates that the hybrid model sig- nificantly outperforms both pure customization and pure personalization approaches, achieving a 95% fault detection rate compared to 75% for customization-only and 88% for personalization-only models. The hybrid approach also showed superior performance in predictive maintenance effectiveness (96%), operational downtime reduction (92%), and cost optimization (90%). The research presents three architectural frameworks for ATSaaS implementation—customization-based, personalization-based, and hybrid—providing a structured approach for different airline categories. Large airlines, with their extensive technical expertise and complex operational requirements, benefit from enhancing their cus- tomized maintenance programs with personalization tools, improving overall maintenance efficiency by 23%. Simultaneously, smaller operators, often constrained by limited re- sources, can use ATSaaS platforms to access sophisticated maintenance capabilities without extensive in-house expertise, reducing operational costs by 35% compared to traditional approaches. The study concludes that the successful integration of customization and personalization through ATSaaS platforms represents a promising direction for optimizing aviation maintenance operations, supporting the industry’s movement toward data-driven, adaptive maintenance solutions. Keywords: aviation maintenance; customization; personalization; aviation technical support as a service; predictive maintenance; digital transformation; MRO services 1. Introduction 1.1. Background and Motivation The aviation industry faces increasing challenges in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft maintenance while minimizing operational disruptions and costs. Traditional maintenance approaches, based primarily on rigid scheduled inspections and reactive fault repairs, often lead to operational inefficiencies through unnecessary preventive main- tenance, extended equipment downtime during inspections, and an inability to prevent unexpected failures between scheduled service intervals. These

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: Article AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems Igor Kabashkin 1,* , Vladimir Perekrestov 2 and Maksim Pivovar 2 1 2 Engineering Faculty, Transport and Telecommunication Institute, Lauvas iela 2, LV-1019 Riga, Latvia Sky Net Technics, Business Center 03, Ras Al-Khaimah B04-223, United Arab Emirates * Correspondence: kiv@tsi.lv Abstract: This study investigates the integration of customization and personalization ap- proaches in aviation maintenance through Aviation Technical Support as a Service (ATSaaS) platform. Through a comprehensive survey of 86 small and medium-sized airlines, com- bined with mathematical modeling of fault detection systems, the study develops and validates a hybrid framework that integrates traditional maintenance approaches with AI-driven solutions. The comparative analysis demonstrates that the hybrid model sig- nificantly outperforms both pure customization and pure personalization approaches, achieving a 95% fault detection rate compared to 75% for customization-only and 88% for personalization-only models. The hybrid approach also showed superior performance in predictive maintenance effectiveness (96%), operational downtime reduction (92%), and cost optimization (90%). The research presents three architectural frameworks for ATSaaS implementation—customization-based, personalization-based, and hybrid—providing a structured approach for different airline categories. Large airlines, with their extensive technical expertise and complex operational requirements, benefit from enhancing their cus- tomized maintenance programs with personalization tools, improving overall maintenance efficiency by 23%. Simultaneously, smaller operators, often constrained by limited re- sources, can use ATSaaS platforms to access sophisticated maintenance capabilities without extensive in-house expertise, reducing operational costs by 35% compared to traditional approaches. The study concludes that the successful integration of customization and personalization through ATSaaS platforms represents a promising direction for optimizing aviation maintenance operations, supporting the industry’s movement toward data-driven, adaptive maintenance solutions. Keywords: aviation maintenance; customization; personalization; aviation technical support as a service; predictive maintenance; digital transformation; MRO services 1. Introduction 1.1. Background and Motivation The aviation industry faces increasing challenges in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft maintenance while minimizing operational disruptions and costs. Traditional maintenance approaches, based primarily on rigid scheduled inspections and reactive fault repairs, often lead to operational inefficiencies through unnecessary preventive main- tenance, extended equipment downtime during inspections, and an inability to prevent unexpected failures between scheduled service intervals. Aircraft Maintenance and Support Services for Airlines The evolution of digital marketplaces in aviation maintenance is transforming how airlines access technical support, predictive analytics, and MRO services. The ATSaaS model facilitates this shift by offering on-demand, AI-driven maintenance solutions through cloud-based platforms, allowing airlines to optimize operations, reduce costs, and improve fleet reliability. By integrating customization and personalization, ATSaaS enables airlines to select tailored maintenance packages while benefiting from AI-driven fault detection, remote diagnostics, and predictive maintenance strategies. ATSaaS improves fault detection capabilities compared to conventional maintenance methods, which often rely on scheduled inspections and reactive repairs. Unlike tradi-tional time-based or condition-based maintenance approaches, ATSaaS uses real-time sensor data, AI-driven anomaly detection, and predictive analytics to identify potential failures before they escalate into critical malfunctions. Key advantages of ATSaaS over conventional methods include • ATSaaS collects and analyzes real-time data from aircraft sensors, enabling instant fault detection and response. • Machine learning models identify subtle anomalies, and degradation patterns that human technicians or rule-based systems may overlook, allowing earlier and more accurate failure predictions. This leads to more targeted the maintenance resources on strategies, focusing they turn components most likely to fail, ultimately leading to optimized maintenance plans [8]. As industrial demands for efficiency and reliability are pushing traditional maintenance methods to their limits, Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) has emerged as a powerful solution in aviation industry [10], in which PdM is also a key element [12]. By analyzing real-time and historical data, PHM can assess aircraft equipment health and predict future issues. Machine learning, a key tool within PHM, utilizes algorithms trained on vast amounts of data to predict equipment health or remaining useful life (RUL) [6]. However, a major hurdle lies in the need for labeled data (data with known outcomes) for training these algorithms [13]. Limited labeled data can significantly restrict the effectiveness of machine learning in PdM [14]. Acquiring the ideal data for machine learning in PdM for aircraft is practically challenging. This data, commonly known as "run-to-failure" data, consists of time-series readings that capture the gradual degradation of a component – from a healthy state all the way to complete failure [15]. The problem lies in the extended timelines involved. For instance, a seemingly minor issue in a critical aircraft part might take months or even years to progress to complete failure under normal operating conditions. However, allowing a component to reach this point is not only unacceptable but also poses a serious safety risk. To prioritize safety, aircraft parts are routinely replaced well before reaching a critical failure state. Researchers are actively addressing this challenge by developing generative AI models that can predict future equipment health even with scarce failure data [16]. In order to improve PdM procedures in the aviation sector and get beyond data constraints, generative AI (GAI) models are an effective technique. These algorithms may create synthetic data that imitates real-world "run-to-failure" circumstances after being trained on available data, even if it is incomplete. This synthetic data can then be used to train other machine learning algorithms for PdM tasks. Therefore, machine learning models may be able to forecast equipment

A variety of methods are included in generative AI, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and variational autoencoders (VAEs). VAEs generate unique and credible data points by examining underlying patterns in the data [8]. GANs, on the other hand, employ a collaborative competition between two neural networks. One network generates data, while the other strives to differentiate between real and generated data. While these reviews offered valuable insights, they lacked a specific focus on the aircraft industry. Similarly, Carvalho et al. Fu et al. [10] reviewed data-driven prognostic algorithms, including conventional and deep learning models. They highlighted aircraft as a key PdM application and reviewed papers using different models for various aircraft equipment. However, their focus was not on generative AI. Similarly, Serradilla et al. [8] reviewed deep learning architectures for PdM stages like anomaly detection and RUL estimation, using aircraft systems as examples. Although they discussed generative models in general but lacked details pertaining to their applications in aircraft PdM. Adryan & Sastra [6] reviewed machine learning for aircraft engine PdM, concluding that LSTM and GRU models deliver high accuracy (over 99%). Abdulrahman et al. Abstract Predictive maintenance (PdM) is a critical tool in aviation, promoting sustainability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. However, a significant challenge in implementing predictive maintenance framework is limited run-to-failure data due to frequent preventive maintenance. Generative AI (GAI) offers a promising solution to this challenge by generating synthetic data, enabling more accurate predictions of aircraft system health. The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: . The recent surge in scientific publications exploring GAI's potential for avi ation predictive maintenance emphasizes the need for a comprehensive review. This research addresses this gap by conducting a systematic literature review (SLR), employing an active learning open-source tool to thoroughly analyze papers sourced from four scientific databases. These papers focus on predictive maintenance of aircraft systems through generative AI models. The findings of this review examine various aspects of GAI-powered predictive maintenance, including its objectives, the diversity of models employed, areas of application, datasets used for model validation, and the prevailing challenges and emerging trends. The review identified that most commonly used GAI models in aircraft PdM are GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks), VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) or combination of these models. However, knowledge sharing, model integration and specificity are the key challenges associated with the implementation of these models. This strong knowledge -base will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners aiming to improve their knowledge and implementation of GAI- driven models for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. Keywords: Generative AI, Predictive Maintenance (PdM), Aircraft Maintenance, Generative Adversarial Network, Data Augmentation 1. Introduction Maintenance plays a pivotal role in the aviation industry, with its importance deeply rooted in ensuring the reliability and safety of aircraft operations. Operating in an environment characterized by extreme temperatures, high altitudes, and various forces during flight, aircraft systems undergo age-related degradation and operational wear without regular maintenance. The repercussions of such neglect are severe, leading to system failures that can compromise the safety of passengers and crew on board [1]. Conversely, the financial ramifications of grounded aircraft due to insufficient maintenance are substantial, compelling airlines to prioritize comprehensive maintenance programs [2]. Regular maintenance actions not only strengthen the structural and operational integrity of aircraft but also extend the lifespan of critical equipment, mitigating revenue losses associated with unplanned downtime [3]. According to a report by Statista, the financial burden of unscheduled aircraft maintenance is anticipated to skyrocket in the aviation industry, reaching an estimated annual cost of nearly 39 billion USD by 2035 [4]. This projection is particularly alarming considering the expected surge in the global fleet size, forecasted to almost double from 25,900 to 47,080 aircraft between 2019 and 2041, due to increased demand for both passenger and cargo transportation [5]. As aging planes with limited sensor capabilities are retired, a significant surge in maintenance requirements is inevitable, potentially overwhelming existing data management systems

content.abstract_or_snippet: questions. The disagreements were carefully reflected upon, and decisions were made accordingly. Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. The research questions themselves might have missed crucial aspects or made assumptions due to the evolving nature of the field and the vast number of approaches for developing these that technologies. Moreover, only considering studies combined GAI with predictive maintenance limited the findings' generalizability. This means they might not be applicable to other forms of predictive maintenance techniques. 6. Conclusion This study explored the growing use of Generative AI in transforming how the aviation industry performs predictive maintenance on aircraft. A systematic literature review was conducted, analyzing studies published over the past five years. The focus was on understanding how GAI is the role revolutionizing aircraft PdM. This generative models play, the specific models being used, the areas where GAI is applied, and the datasets employed to validate these models. included The study implemented a semi-automated approach for the literature review, utilizing ASReview and PRISMA techniques. This process identified 16 relevant studies out of a pool of 442. The findings highlight the significant contribution of GAI in surpassing traditional PdM methods. Notably, GAI excels at addressing limitations caused by a lack of data. This allows for more accurate diagnoses of equipment degradation and the ability to estimate the RUL of aircraft systems with greater precision. The primary objective of utilizing GAI in aircraft PdM is to generate realistic RUL data. This data is then used to predict RUL and diagnose faults. GAI proves particularly adept at tackling challenges like imbalanced and noisy data, information on normal operations significantly where outweighs data on faults. GANs, VAE-GANs, and various combinations of these models are the most prevalent GAI approaches used for predicting aircraft component RUL. the Across reviewed studies, NASA's CMAPSS emerged as the most popular dataset for validating GAI- based PdM models. However, a limitation of this dataset is its focus on turbofan engines. This restricts the possibilities for conducting research on PdM for other aircraft components. 7. References [1] Marais, K. By integrating correlation-based feature selection with tree classifiers and deep learn- ing techniques, the ATSaaS predictive maintenance model achieved higher fault detection rates, reduced false positives, and improved predictive accuracy. This hybrid approach ensures that maintenance interventions are both data-driven and operationally reliable, minimizing downtime and optimizing resource allocation. 3.6. Results of AI-Based Fault Detection Modeling The modeling and evaluation of the customization-based, personalization-based, and hybrid AI-based fault detection models were conducted to assess their performance across key maintenance parameters: fault detection rate FDR, predictive maintenance effectiveness PME, operational downtime reduction ODR and cost optimization CO. The results highlight the comparative advantages of each approach and demonstrate the hybrid model’s superior performance in predictive maintenance applications. Figure 27 presents a comparative breakdown of performance scores for each model across the four key evaluation criteria. The results indicate significant variations in the effectiveness of different AI-based fault detection approaches. The Customization Model achieved an FDR of 75%, demonstrating its effectiveness in structured, rule-based detection but lacked adaptability to unforeseen conditions. The Personalization Model used machine learning-based predictive analytics, achieving an FDR of 88% and a significantly higher PME of 92%, indicating its strong real-time fault prediction capabilities. The Hybrid Model consistently outperformed both approaches, achieving 95% FDR, 96% PME, 92% ODR, and 90% CO, demonstrating its opti- mal balance between structured rule-based logic and AI-driven predictive analytics. These results confirm that while Customization provides precise control, and Personalization Processes 2025, 13, 666 36 of 54 enhances predictive capabilities, the Hybrid Model delivers the most effective combination of fault classification accuracy and maintenance optimization. Figure 27. Comparative analysis of AI-based fault detection models. The superior performance of the hybrid ATSaaS model was validated using a com- prehensive dataset collected from 86 small and medium-sized airlines, covering a diverse range of operational conditions, aircraft types, and maintenance records. The dataset includes real-time sensor data, historical maintenance logs, and operational performance metrics, ensuring a robust basis for model evaluation. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: ] proposed a novel approach for diagnosing faults in aviation hydraulic pumps. They addressed the scarcity of fault samples and integrates local wavelet similarity fusion (LWSF) with a CNN in a method called LWSF_CNN. It works by decomposing vibration signals into wavelet packet coefficients (WPCs) which contain characteristic information at specific frequencies. LWSF then modifies the WPCs of faulty samples by referencing normal samples, ensuring the generated samples remain representative of the fault. Finally, the modified WPCs are used to reconstruct new, augmented faulty samples, increasing the diversity and number of samples for improved fault classification. The CNN is then trained on this balanced dataset and is used to diagnose faults in unseen test samples. 3.5 in GAI based Aircraft PdM? RQ-3: Which application domains exist Recent years have seen a surge in innovative methods for PdM in aviation sector, with many researchers employing a combination of data-driven analysis and machine learning techniques. Below is an overview of the various PdM applications within the aircraft industry. Aero-engine and rolling bearings have been identified to be the leading applications in aircraft where GAI has reached a more mature stage. 3.5.1 Aero-Engine. Aircraft engines are complex machinery and a significant expense for airlines, requiring regular maintenance to ensure smooth operation. These engines rely on a network of sensors to monitor their health, including factors like fan inlet temperature, pressure, and rotational speed. Aircraft engine failures can be catastrophic, so ensuring their reliability is crucial [42]. Predicting RUL of an aircraft engine involves analyzing complex multi- variate time series data of sensors collected throughout the engine's lifecycle, which makes it as one of the most challenging tasks in PHM [37]. Zhang et al. [27] describe the three stages of an engine’s life in their paper: healthy, initial degradation, and severe degradation. The healthy stage has no degradation trends and random sensor readings. The initial degradation stage, however, holds the key to identifying impending failure, but data from this stage is scarce [27, 36]. Existing methods that do not differentiate these stages can lead to false alarms or even accidents. Majority of papers have talked about the challenges which are faced in acquiring degradation data of aircraft. Obtaining enough information on complete engine failures is a significant hurdle. It could take years for an engine such as turbofan engine to naturally degrade from its peak performance to a complete breakdown. In practical scenarios, experiencing failure is highly unfavorable as it poses safety risks

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: the maintenance needs of air- lines with fleets of varying sizes, making them ideal for both small and large operators. Predictive analytics allow issues to be identified and addressed before they escalate, minimizing downtime and improving fleet reliability. Despite its advantages, personalization also has challenges: The effectiveness of personalization relies heavily on advanced technologies and accurate data inputs, making it susceptible to disruptions if technical issues arise. Initial investments in ATSaaS platforms and staff training may pose challenges for resource-constrained airlines. • While personalization dynamically tailors services, it may not fully address the highly specific, predefined needs that customization can accommodate. The last thesis can be commented on further. One of the primary limitations of personalization-based maintenance approaches is their dependence on accurate and un- interrupted data streams from aircraft sensors and operational databases. Inconsistent or erroneous data can lead to false alarms, misclassifications, or delayed maintenance actions, potentially affecting fleet availability and operational efficiency. To mitigate these risks, the following strategies can be implemented: • • • Implementing multi-source data validation techniques, such as cross-referencing sensor readings with historical maintenance logs and operational benchmarks, ensures higher data reliability and accuracy. Additionally, redundant sensor networks and failover mechanisms help maintain data continuity in case of hardware malfunctions. AI-driven anomaly detection algorithms can identify inconsistencies in incoming data, flagging abnormal sensor behavior and preventing incorrect fault predictions. This enhances the robustness of predictive maintenance models by filtering out faulty or misleading data points. Combining machine learning-based predictive analytics with rule-based verification systems can balance the flexibility of AI-driven models with structured decision- making frameworks, reducing the likelihood of false positives and unverified alerts. Processes 2025, 13, 666 41 of 54 By integrating these strategies, personalization-based predictive maintenance mod- els can achieve greater reliability, minimizing risks associated with inaccurate data and operational disruptions. While customization and personalization are distinct, they are not mutually exclu- sive. Integrating these approaches into a hybrid model offers opportunities to use their respective strengths. For large airlines, customization can remain the foundation of long-term mainte- nance strategies, while personalization tools enhance real-time adaptability and predictive capabilities. For SMAs, personalization can serve as the primary approach, with limited customiza- tion applied to address high-priority needs or unique regulatory requirements. The comparison underscores that neither approach is universally superior; their ef- fectiveness depends on the airline’s size, operational complexity, and resource availability. Customization excels in providing control and precision

content.abstract_or_snippet: serves as the system’s core intelligence, whereas the Decision En-gine balances inputs from both the Rule Processor (handling human-defined rules) and AI Analytics (processing automated data). The Conflict Resolution module harmonizes Processes 2025, 13, 666 23 of 54 Service Processing translates the hybrid decisions into actionable plans through a structured workflow: The task Generator creates specific maintenance tasks, the Schedule Optimizer arranges them efficiently, the Resource Allocator assigns necessary resources, and the Implementation Plan provides detailed execution guidelines. The Feedback System ensures continuous improvement through Performance Track- ing, User Feedback, and System Analytics, generating Optimization Suggestions that feed back into both customization and personalization components. The User Interface Layer provides a Configuration Dashboard where maintenance teams can view both Man- ual Settings and AI Recommendations, with a Validation Interface ensuring oversight of all decisions. This hybrid architecture offers several unique advantages: Balanced integration of human expertise and AI capabilities. Conflict resolution between automated and manual inputs. Continuous optimization through comprehensive feedback loops. Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Stakeholder Questionnaire The aircraft maintenance stakeholder questionnaire was developed as a key com- ponent of the study to gain insights into the perspectives, practices, and expectations of various stakeholders involved in aircraft maintenance. This survey aimed to gather data on the adoption and implementation of ATSaaS platforms, with a particular focus on the integration of customization and personalization in maintenance processes. Un- derstanding stakeholder experiences and preferences is critical for identifying challenges, assessing the effectiveness of existing practices, and shaping the future development of ATSaaS platforms. The questionnaire was designed to capture a comprehensive view of the ecosystem by addressing the needs and roles of key stakeholders, including airlines, MRO providers

Flexible adaptation to both routine and exceptional maintenance scenarios. • • • • • Maintained human oversight while using automation benefits. The system’s strength lies in its ability to use both human expertise and artificial intelligence while maintaining clear communication channels between all components. The feedback loops ensure continuous improvement of both automated and manual processes, while the validation interfaces maintain the necessary human oversight. However, the success of this hybrid approach depends on the effective integration of its components and clear protocols for resolving conflicts between human and AI-generated decisions. The system must maintain a delicate balance between automation efficiency and human control, ensuring that safety and regulatory requirements are never compromised. This architecture represents the future of aviation maintenance, where human expertise and artificial intelligence work in harmony to deliver optimal maintenance solutions. The hybrid approach ensures that neither the human element nor the technological capabilities are compromised but rather enhanced through their integration. 3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Stakeholder Questionnaire The aircraft maintenance stakeholder questionnaire was developed as a key com- ponent of the study to gain insights into the perspectives, practices, and expectations of various stakeholders involved in aircraft maintenance. This survey aimed to gather data on the adoption and implementation of ATSaaS platforms, with a particular focus on the integration of customization and personalization in maintenance processes. Un- derstanding stakeholder experiences and preferences is critical for identifying challenges, assessing the effectiveness of existing practices, and shaping the future development of ATSaaS platforms. The questionnaire was designed to capture a comprehensive view of the ecosystem by addressing the needs and roles of key stakeholders, including airlines, MRO providers

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: records frequently include errors [Mark Roboff, SkyThread CEO, 2023]. 4. Data Ownership In addition to data availability and quality, there is some question about data ownership. Many OEMs restrict access to operational data for airlines and MROs (Ref. 16, 33). Finally, pilot unions sometimes negotiate limitations on how the data can be used or requirements to anonymize data [Amy O’Dell, United, 2023]. Conversely, it can also be diffi- cult for the Engine OEM to get access to operator’s essential data (e.g. data regarding EHM, flight connections, on-wing maintenance etc.). These data limitations or data anonymizations sometimes make it difficult to use the data for predictive maintenance. Finally, labeled relevant degradation datasets are essential for model building and validation in academia and industry. This can be seen in the impact of existing datasets like the CMAPSS datasets (Ref. 34, 35). 7.2 Investments Needed A few contributions that could help close this barrier are described below. They are clustered into those that aid in accessing more data and those that improve the way data is dealt with. 7.2.1 Accessing more Data 1. Develop New Sensing Technologies New sensing technologies (e.g., embedded or printed sensors or active sensing techniques) can help generate data relevant for prognostics that may not exist now. [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023] 2. Data Export from Aircraft There is ongoing discussion and first approaches among some airlines and airports to explore technologies and methods for exporting data from aircraft (e.g., gate link and satellite) to secure and accessible storage. Further work is needed in order to make use of the data for predictive maintenance operations, model building, and tool validation. [Mark Roboff, SkyThread, 2023][Amy O’Dell, United, 2023][Madhav Misra, RISE, 2023] 3. Digitize Maintenance Records Already underway by many airlines and MROs, mainte- nance records should be standardized, digitized, and parameterised (for machine readability) so they can be available for predictive maintenance model building and validation. [Madhav Misra, RISE, 2023][Marc Roboff, SkyThread, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Deutsch- land, 2023] 4. Develop and Mature Methods and Technologies for Automated Inspection Mature at-gate

Processes 2025, 13, 666 https://doi.org/10.3390/pr13030666 Processes 2025, 13, 666 2 of 54 significant operational disruptions and cost overruns [1]. The growing complexity of mod- ern aviation systems necessitates the integration of advanced fault detection and control mechanisms to optimize maintenance processes and enhance operational resilience. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and Internet of Things (IoT) sensor technologies have enabled a shift toward predictive maintenance and condition-based maintenance (CBM) strategies. These methodologies use real-time data collection, intelligent fault classification, and automated control processes to detect early signs of degradation, classify anomalies, and predict failures before they result in costly malfunctions. To fully utilize these capabilities, the Aviation Technical Support as a Service (ATSaaS) platform was proposed [1]. It integrates AI-driven predictive analytics into its core architecture, enabling scalable and adaptive maintenance solutions. By incorporating real-time fault detection and automated decision-making, ATSaaS enhances traditional maintenance frameworks, optimizing operational efficiency and minimizing downtime for both large airlines and smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. content.abstract_or_snippet: high- light the need for continued investigation into aviation maintenance optimization. They also underscore the importance of considering practical implementation factors alongside theoretical frameworks when developing maintenance solutions for the aviation industry. 4.9. Future Research Directions The integration of customization and personalization in aircraft maintenance repre- sents a significant shift in how airlines and service providers manage operational efficiency and safety. While the current frameworks of traditional MRO models and ATSaaS platforms have demonstrated their value, further research is needed to explore emerging technolo- gies, address implementation challenges, and unlock new possibilities for innovation in this domain. Advanced predictive analytics represents a critical area for future studies, focusing on enhancing maintenance capabilities through machine learning and artificial intelligence. This includes developing more accurate failure prediction algorithms for complex systems, Processes 2025, 13, 666 50 of 54 integrating environmental and operational data for nuanced predictive models, and inves- tigating hybrid analytics frameworks that combine predictive and prescriptive approaches for optimized maintenance scheduling. Research into the integration of customization and personalization requires deeper exploration, particularly in developing hybrid models that effectively combine the precision of customization with the adaptability of personalization. Key areas include identifying optimal frameworks for integrating manual customization with data-driven personal- ization, assessing operational and economic impacts on airlines of different sizes, and developing methodologies for dynamically transitioning between approaches based on real-time conditions. The scalability of ATSaaS platforms warrants further investigation as these systems gain broader adoption. Research should focus on evaluating how platforms can efficiently scale predictive maintenance capabilities for large fleets without compromising accuracy, in- vestigating cost-effective solutions for small and medium-sized airlines, and exploring mod- ular design approaches that enable seamless adaptation to growing operational demands. Cybersecurity in aircraft maintenance emerges as a crucial research priority given the increasing reliance on digital tools and data-driven platforms. Studies should assess vulnerabilities in ATSaaS platforms and maintenance technologies, investigate blockchain technology’s potential for enhancing data security and traceability, and design robust contingency plans and resilience frameworks to mitigate cyber risks. Economic viability and return on investment analysis require thorough investigation through cost-benefit analyses of different maintenance models, assessment of ROI for airlines transitioning to ATSaaS platforms, and exploration of financial incentives and business models to encourage adoption among smaller operators. Future research in aircraft maintenance should ultimately aim to enhance the inte- gration of advanced technologies, refine hybrid models, and address challenges related to scalability, cybersecurity, and sustainability. These efforts will contribute to developing innovative, efficient, and environmentally responsible maintenance practices that meet the evolving demands of the aviation industry while improving

They explored various security, demonstrating domains generative AI's potential. However, their work lacked an aviation industry focus, highlighting the gap in research on generative AI for predictive maintenance in the aviation sector. In essence, existing systematic reviews offer valuable insights into PdM using various techniques, but they lack a dedicated focus on generative AI models within the specific context of aviation predictive maintenance. This gap presents a compelling opportunity for further research to explore the potential and unique functionalities of generative AI in enhancing predictive maintenance practices for the aviation industry. Keeping in view the above stated facts, to the best of author’s knowledge, this study stands out from previous research in two ways. First, it strictly followed a well- established method called SLR protocol. Second, it narrowed the focus to the specific combination of generative AI and predictive maintenance within the aviation industry. By focusing on a specific niche and employing a systematic approach, this study delivers unique and valuable insights. 1.2 Paper Organization This paper offers a comprehensive review of GAI based PdM in aviation industry, following a structured approach. Chapter 2 outlines the SLR protocol in detail, which combines both manual and semi-automated approaches. Chapter 3 describes the findings of the SLR. It begins by presenting key statistics of the SLR sample, followed by comprehensive exploration of each research question. Chapter 4 dives into a discussion. Subsequently, in chapter 6 the significance of this research is presented and concluding remarks are summarized. 2. Research Methodology A semi-automated SLR was conducted to collect relevant primary literature. While broader keywords might capture a wider range of studies, they risk including irrelevant content. Conversely, overly specific terms could exclude valuable research. In this context, several key factors were considered when finalizing search string for this SLR. A thorough initial search of keywords was initiated with broader terms like "generative artificial intelligence" and "predictive maintenance" in the aviation domain. However, these terms yielded a vast number of results that lacked the specific focus desired. For instance, the search term "generative artificial intelligence" included studies on various deep learning and machine learning models, which wasn’t relevant to the specific research interest of thesis centered on generative AI models used for predicting aircraft maintenance needs. To address this challenge, a keyword refinement process was carried out. A preliminary review of existing literature was conducted, carefully examining the titles, abstracts, and keywords of relevant articles. This analysis helped identify synonyms, abbreviations, and related terms that could be incorporated into the search string. A crucial metric of predictive maintenance, "Remaining Useful Life," was added. Additionally, interchangeable terms related to Prognostics and Health Management, and "prognostics," were also added. Terms related to generative AI such as "generative," "adversarial," "GAN," "VAE," and "variational autoencoder" were included. Finally, aviation, aero, and aircraft were added to ensure the research focused on the aviation sector. We looked at over 400 research papers from 2019 and narrowed it down to 16 high-quality studies. Furthermore, lack of interpretability causes lack of trust in the output of GAI models especially for safety critical systems, as a lesser understanding generally exists into how these models arrive at their conclusion. Also, GAI models trained on specific aircraft system might not work well with other systems, due to sensor data variations. Finally, the implementation of generative models in PdM of aircraft is affected by high-dimensional aircraft data, expensive real-time integration challenges. To achieve this goal effectively, a structured methodology is crucial. Kitchenham & Charters [19] breaks down the process of conducting a systematic review into three key phases: Planning, conducting and reporting of reviews. The entire review protocol of this research has therefore been constructed on guidelines of Kitchenham & Charters [19] and Van Dinter et al. [20]. Figure 1 represents the review protocol for this study. 2.1 Research Questions This study aims to find all relevant information on applying generative AI for predictive maintenance in the aviation industry. To effectively integrate generative AI, three key areas require investigation: (1) its purpose and extent of its utilization, (2) development approaches for generative AI-based predictive maintenance algorithms, and (3) anticipated challenges in implementation. To address these areas, research questions were formulated: following the • RQ1: What is the role of Generative AI in aircraft PdM? • RQ2: Which Generative models are used in Aircraft PdM? • RQ3: Which application domains exist in Generative AI based Aircraft PdM? • RQ4: Which datasets are used to validate Generative AI based models in aircraft PdM? Limitations of Research it and testing refinement; however, Although the search protocol was formulated after thorough is acknowledged that the search for relevant papers might have excluded some valuable studies. Phrasing search queries can be tricky, and even minor wording changes can significantly alter search results in different databases. Furthermore, the search of relevant studies was concluded in Feb 2024, excluding any research published afterwards. This is a challenge in a rapidly developing field like generative AI. With so many new publications, capturing all relevant literature up to the review's submission date is difficult. Even with efficient research methods, there's always a time gap between data collection and the final review. This means there might be recently published techniques that address the issues identified in this review, but they weren't included due to the cutoff date. This can potentially lead to missing important research on the topic. Another limitation is publication bias. Studies with positive results are more likely to be published than those with negative findings. The authors tried to address this by focusing on high-quality research using specific methods, but there's still a chance relevant information was missed. Selection bias is also a concern. Even with predetermined criteria for including studies, subjective judgments can still influence which ones the research area, are chosen. The broad scope of encompassing both predictive maintenance and generative AI, requires expertise in various fields. This increases the possibility of misinterpreting some studies during the selection process. The data extraction and synthesis process were equally shared between the authors to capture important information needed to answer the research questions. The disagreements were carefully reflected upon, and decisions were made accordingly. Finally, the study acknowledges limitations in its internal and external validity. While the dotted line depicts a slow and steady increase in relevant studies found through a random manual search, the solid line representing the active learning process demonstrates a much steeper rise. This signifies that the active learning method identifies relevant studies at a significantly faster rate. 3.3 RQ-1: What is the role of Generative AI in Aircraft PdM? Aircraft rely on healthy systems for safe and efficient operations, and it is identified that predictive maintenance based on GAI plays a crucial role in capturing the faults before they even occur. Generative AI offers a powerful suite of techniques to address the challenges of imbalanced and scarce data for capturing the early stages of aircraft system degradation. Hence, the primary roles of generative AI are s d r o c e r t n a v e e r l f o r e b m u N 55 46 37 28 18 9 0 1 Active Learning Manual Learning 25 49 74 98 122 Number of reviewed records Fig. 8 Active Learning Vs Manual Learning (1) data augmentation, (2) feature extraction and (3) missing data imputation. Data augmentation refers to generation of new data and augmenting it with existing data. The goal of data augmentation is to increase data quantity and diversity [27]. Moreover, GAI can be used to extract key features of the data for building classifiers and predictive models, hence assisting in learning data representation [28]. Finally, data imputation techniques involve filling in the blanks in the data. Unlike traditional estimation techniques of statistical analysis, neural network methods work in two stages. First, they train a model on the data. Then, in the imputation stage, the trained model is used to "fill in the blanks" for missing values by generating data that follows those learned patterns [29]. Aircraft generate a lot of data through sensors installed but the key problem in implementing PdM is that the data is imbalanced. This means that normal operation readings significantly outnumber anomaly data. This imbalance mirrors situations where the number of healthy samples vastly outweighs defective ones, or the training data fails to represent all fault categories equally. Consequently, GAI models can become biased towards the over-represented normal class, leading to misleading performance metrics like accuracy. To address this challenge, various techniques are being used to balance the data before training the models. This involved oversampling the rare "failure" data or under sampling the abundant "normal" data. Wu et al. [30],

Das rechtliche Rahmenwerk ermöglicht eine Vielzahl an Ausgestaltungen, die Reform des PBefG aus dem Jahr 2021 scheint in dieser Hinsicht gelungen. Die primäre Hürde für On-Demand-Ridepooling besteht derzeit im hohen Bedarf an Fahrpersonal und den damit verbundenen Kosten. Auf- grund des sich derzeit durch den demographischen Wandel zuspitzenden Fachkräfte- mangels und auslaufender Förderprojekte könnten die kommenden Jahre auch von einer starken Reduktion der in Betrieb befindlichen Systeme geprägt sein. Eine Her- ausforderung stellt in diesem Kontext das Deutschlandticket dar, da es die Einnah- meseite des ÖPNV bei Dauerkunden deutlich senkt. Im gebündelten Verkehr kann dies teilweise durch höhere Fahrgastzahlen ausgeglichen werden, bei flexiblen Be- dienformen führt eine höhere Nachfrage schnell zu einem deutlichen Kostenanstieg. Daher ist davon auszugehen, dass die vor der Einführung des Deutschlandtickets an- gesetzten Komfortzuschläge in der kommenden Zeit deutlich steigen (müssen). Wirklich wirtschaftlich, funktional und ökologisch werden die Systeme erst, wenn ein substanzieller Anteil des Modal Split auf diese neue Mobilitätsform entfällt. Denn erst dann ermöglicht die Vielzahl der Anfragen und Fahrten hohe Poolingquoten auch ohne große Umwege und Wartezeiten. Dies ist aufgrund der Kostenstruktur und des Mangels an Fahrer*innen erst mit vollautomatisierten bzw. autonomen Fahrzeugen möglich. Andererseits gilt auch: Ab einem gewissen Fahrgastaufkommen ist eine gebündelte Beförderung sowohl ökologischer als auch ökonomischer. Daher stellt insbesondere eine Kombination von hoch getakteten Schnellverbindungen des Linienverkehrs mit autonomen Zubringershuttles für die letzte Meile eine interes- sante Möglichkeit für einen attraktiven öffentlichen Verkehr der Zukunft dar, der substanzielle Anteile des Modal Splits erreichen könnte. This flexible pricing structure allows airlines to optimize their maintenance costs while ensuring access to high-quality technical support services when needed. 2.4. Aircraft Maintenance and Support Services for Airlines The evolution of digital marketplaces in aviation maintenance is transforming how airlines access technical support, predictive analytics, and MRO services. The ATSaaS model facilitates this shift by offering on-demand, AI-driven maintenance solutions through cloud-based platforms, allowing airlines to optimize operations, reduce costs, and improve fleet reliability. By integrating customization and personalization, ATSaaS enables airlines to select tailored maintenance packages while benefiting from AI-driven fault detection, remote diagnostics, and predictive maintenance strategies. ATSaaS improves fault detection capabilities compared to conventional maintenance methods, which often rely on scheduled inspections and reactive repairs. Unlike tradi-tional time-based or condition-based maintenance approaches, ATSaaS uses real-time sensor data, AI-driven anomaly detection, and predictive analytics to identify potential failures before they escalate into critical malfunctions. Key advantages of ATSaaS over conventional methods include • ATSaaS collects and analyzes real-time data from aircraft sensors, enabling instant fault detection and response. • Machine learning models identify subtle anomalies, and degradation patterns that human technicians or rule-based systems may overlook, allowing earlier and more accurate failure predictions.
